```mermaid
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Fastening Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Traditional Boat Fastening Methods] --> F1[Sewn Plank - Zanzibar Mtepe]
    F --> F2[Carvel Nailed - Iranian Baggala]
    F --> F3[Clinker Riveted - UK Lugger]
    F --> F4[Carvel Bulkhead - Hong Kong Junk]
    F1 --> F1a[Coir Rope Through Drilled Holes]
    F1 --> F1b[Resin Lime Paste for Sealing]
    F2 --> F2a[Iron Nails into Frames]
    F2 --> F2b[Wooden Treenails for Strength]
    F3 --> F3a[Copper Rivets Through Overlap]
    F3 --> F3b[Clenched Iron Nails]
    F4 --> F4a[Iron Nails to Frames]
    F4 --> F4b[Wooden Bulkhead Planks Sealed with Putty]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Construction Process Sequence (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Traditional Boat Building] --> P1[Step 1 Select Site and Timber]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2 Lay Keel]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Set Stem and Sternpost]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Install Internal Frames]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Apply Outer Planking]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Caulk and Seal Seams]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Fit Deck and Mast Step]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Rig Sails and Masts]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Launch into Water]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When the question says *"Explain the construction of a traditional boat"*, follow this exact 9-step sequence. The examiner allocates marks across these stages. Skipping the *caulking and sealing* step costs you 2 marks instantly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question] *With a neat sketch, describe the construction features of the Mtepe boat of Zanzibar.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Mtepe is a traditional Swahili sailing boat of Zanzibar, distinguished by its **sewn-plank construction using coir rope** instead of metal nails. The planks are first soaked in water for flexibility, then bent around a keel-and-frame skeleton. Conical holes are drilled along the edges of adjacent planks, and a long needle threads **coir (coconut-husk) rope** through these holes, binding the planks together. The seams are then sealed with a paste of dammar resin, lime, and coconut fiber. The boat is **double-ended** (symmetric bow and stern) and carries a **triangular lateen sail**. This elastic, monocoque-like construction allows the hull to flex with wave loading, making it ideal for Indian Ocean voyages. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question] *Compare the sail rigs of an Iranian Baggala and a Hong Kong junk.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Iranian Baggala** uses the **lateen rig** — a triangular sail set on a long yard that slopes upward from the masthead to the deck. It typically has two masts and is well-suited to downwind sailing in the Persian Gulf.
The **Hong Kong junk**, by contrast, uses the **battened lug rig** — a four-sided sail supported by 4 to 10 horizontal bamboo battens, suspended from a horizontal yard. Each batten can be independently trimmed, giving the junk superior upwind ability and easier reefing. Thus, the Baggala is optimized for steady monsoon trade winds, while the junk is optimized for variable South China Sea conditions. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
> *a) Explain the construction of a traditional Thames barge of the United Kingdom, highlighting its hull form, planking method, and rig. (7 marks)*
> *b) Describe the Iranian Lenj boat tradition, with reference to its cultural significance and the types of vessels produced. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Thames Barge Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Hull form (2 marks):** The Thames barge has a flat-bottomed, hard-chine hull with a *lee-board* on each side (instead of a deep keel) to resist leeway. The bow is raked forward, and the stern is wide and rounded — optimized for sailing the shallow Thames Estuary.
2. **Planking method (3 marks):** Planking is **carvel** (edge-to-edge), with each plank individually fastened to a heavy oak frame. The garboards (lowest planks) are thick, with thinner planks higher up. Seams are caulked with oakum and sealed with putty or tar.
3. **Sprit rig (2 marks):** The Thames barge carries the iconic **sprit rig** — a huge gaff-rigged mainsail hoisted on a tall mast, plus a *jib* (staysail) forward and a *mizzen* aft on the *stump* of a mainmast. Three crew (master, mate, boy) could handle it.

**(b) Iranian Lenj Tradition (7 marks):**

1. **Cultural significance (3 marks):** *Lenj* is more than boat-building; it is a *collective ritual* of design, construction, sea-launching, and sailing. The tradition is centered in the southern Iranian provinces of Hormozgan, Bushehr, and Sistan-Baluchestan. It was inscribed on **UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2011**, recognizing the oral poetry, traditional music, and communal labor that accompany each boat's construction.
2. **Vessel types (2 marks):** The major vessels include the **Baggala** (large two-masted cargo dhow), the **Ghanja** (smaller single-mast coastal vessel), the **Sanbuq** (fast pearling craft), and the **Jalbut** (motor-sail hybrid).
3. **Materials and design (2 marks):** Teak is imported from India, while local mangrove is used for frames. Planks are carvel-laid, fastened with iron nails and wooden treenails, and the hull ends in a *high, ornamented transom* with painted decoration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q4.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
> *a) Describe in detail the construction and structural features of a Hong Kong Cantonese junk, including the unique junk-rig sail system. (7 marks)*
> *b) Compare and contrast the construction of the Zanzibar Mtepe and the UK Cornish lugger. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Hong Kong Cantonese Junk (7 marks):**

1. **Hull form (2 marks):** The Cantonese junk has a broad, full-bodied hull with a *transom stern* and a *clipper or raked bow*. The bottom is often round, transitioning to a hard chine near the deck. Watertight *bulkheads* divide the interior into 4–8 compartments — a damage-control feature.
2. **Planking and framing (2 marks):** The hull is **carvel-planked** with hardwood planks, each fastened to a heavy internal frame using iron nails. Caulking is done with putty and tung oil.
3. **Junk-rig sail (3 marks):** The rig consists of a *tall mast*, a *horizontal yard (boom)*, and a *fully battened sail* with 4–10 horizontal bamboo battens. The sail is raised and lowered by halyards; sheets from each batten's end allow precise trim. Reefing is done by simply lowering the sail against the yard — a quick, safe operation.

**(b) Comparison — Mtepe vs Cornish Lugger (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Zanzibar Mtepe | UK Cornish Lugger |
|---|---|---|
| **Plank method** | Sewn with coir rope (no nails) | Clinker overlap (rivets/nails) |
| **Hull form** | Double-ended, symmetric | Sharp stem, asymmetric |
| **Frame** | Light internal frames | Light frames, plank is primary |
| **Fastener** | Coir rope, elastic | Copper rivets, rigid |
| **Flexibility** | Highly flexible (elastic skin) | Moderate (overlapping planks) |
| **Sail** | Triangular lateen | Four-sided lug |
| **Heritage focus** | UNESCO tentative | UK National Historic Fleet |

The Mtepe relies on **elasticity and skin strength** (monocoque behavior), while the lugger relies on **rigid overlapping planking**. Both designs achieve hull strength with light internal framing, but by *opposite* physical principles — *tension-and-bend* (Mtepe) vs *compression-and-shear* (lugger). *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** confuse the *Mtepe* with a *Baggala* — the Mtepe is the *sewn* one, the Baggala is *nailed*.
> - **Do not** call the Hong Kong junk's rig a "lateen" — it is a *battened lug* (or junk rig); lateen is a Zanzibar/Iranian feature.
> - **Always** name the wood species (teak, mangrove, oak) — examiners reward specific vocabulary.
> - **Always** draw a labelled cross-section in Part B questions — it earns 1–2 easy marks.
> - **Avoid** writing "boats of these regions are old-fashioned" — instead, frame the answer around *engineering logic* and *cultural continuity* (Bloom's Level: Evaluate).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1**

- **Zanzibar** = Indian Ocean dhow belt → **Mtepe** is sewn with **coir rope**; **Baggala, Ghanja, Jahazi** are also important.
- **Iran** = Persian Gulf *Lenj* tradition → **UNESCO 2011**; **Baggala, Ghanja, Sanbuq, Jalbut**; **teak wood imported from India**.
- **UK** = North Atlantic → **Clinker vs Carvel** is the central distinction; **Thames Barge (sprit rig), Cornish Lugger, Yorkshire Coble, Norfolk Wherry**.
- **Hong Kong** = South China Sea → **Cantonese junk** with **battened lug sail**; **Sampan, Sanpan, Walla-walla, Tanka boat**; **watertight bulkheads** are a signature feature.
- **Sail types to remember:** *Lateen* (Zanzibar, Iran), *Lug* (UK), *Sprit* (Thames Barge), *Battened lug / junk rig* (Hong Kong).
- **Fastening methods to remember:** *Coir rope* (Mtepe), *Iron nails + treenails* (Iran, HK), *Copper rivets* (UK Clinker).
- **Woods to remember:** *Mangrove, Teak* (tropical); *Oak, Elm, Larch* (UK).
- **UNESCO recognition:** Lenj boats (2011) is the only directly inscribed item in this module.
- **Comparative reasoning** is the highest-weight cognitive skill in KTU 2024 — practice 5-column comparison tables.
- **Engineering take-home:** Sewn-plank Mtepe = monocoque flex; Clinker = overlapping rigid; Carvel = frame-dependent; Junk = bulkhead-compartmentalized. *Four solutions to the same problem — resisting wave load.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yard Selection, Workshop/Yard Requirements, Budgeting and Costs, Project Management, Outsourcing Parts, Buy or Lease of Machinery and Tools, Bill of Materials and Detailed Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Yard Selection, Workshop/Yard Requirements, Budgeting and Costs, Project Management, Outsourcing Parts, Buy or Lease of Machinery and Tools, Bill of Materials and Detailed Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Yard Selection — Formal Definition

In traditional boat building, a **Yard** is the designated physical location (either an open-air slipway, a covered workshop, or a combination of both) where a vessel is constructed, repaired, or maintained. **Yard Selection** is the strategic decision-making process of identifying, evaluating, and finalizing the most suitable site considering geographical, infrastructural, economic, regulatory, and operational factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Yard selection is the foundational pre-project activity that determines downstream feasibility, cost, and timeline of any boat-building project. It is a multi-criteria decision problem.

### 1.1.1 Key Selection Parameters
- **Proximity to water** (sea/river/backwater) — minimum **100 m** preferred for Kerala backwater construction.
- **Slope/gradient** of land for launching — typically between **1:10 to 1:15** for traditional wooden vessels.
- **Soil bearing capacity** — minimum **50 kN/m²** for cradling heavy hulls.
- **Access roads** for timber and machinery transport.
- **Availability of skilled labour** (traditional carpenters — *Aasans*, caulkers).
- **Climatic shelter** — covered workshop preferred for monsoon-sensitive woodwork.

> [!NOTE]
> For Kerala, traditional boat yards are typically located in **Kochi, Beypore, Azhikode, Chombal, and Thiruvananthapuram** coastal belts where teak, anjili, and rosewood are historically sourced.

---

## 1.2 Workshop/Yard Requirements — Formal Definition

**Workshop Requirements** refer to the comprehensive inventory of physical infrastructure, utilities, tools, machinery, safety systems, and human resources that a boat-building yard must possess to execute a project efficiently and safely.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a boat-building yard like a **hospital operating theatre**. Just as a surgery theatre needs sterile rooms, specific lighting, surgical tools, an anaesthetic machine, trained staff, and recovery rooms, a boat yard needs a **mould loft**, **timber seasoning shed**, **machining bay**, **assembly slipway**, **painting dock**, **tool crib**, and **launching gantry** — each performing a specialized function in the "operation" of building a boat.

### 1.2.2 Functional Zones of a Traditional Boat Yard

| Zone | Function | Typical Area (for 20 m vessel) |
|------|----------|-------------------------------|
| **Mould Loft** | Lofting full-scale drawings | 400–600 m² |
| **Timber Storage** | Seasoning & preservation | 200–300 m² |
| **Machining Bay** | Sawing, planing, shaping | 150–250 m² |
| **Assembly Slipway** | Hull construction | 500–800 m² |
| **Caulking Bay** | Sealing joints | 80–100 m² |
| **Painting Dock** | Priming, antifouling | 200 m² |
| **Launching Gantry** | Vessel launch to water | As per vessel size |

---

## 1.3 Budgeting and Costs — Formal Definition

**Budgeting** is the systematic estimation, allocation, and control of financial resources required to complete a boat-building project. It converts the technical scope of work into a **monetary roadmap** with itemized line-items, contingency reserves, and cash-flow schedules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Costing Principle:** In traditional boat building, **labour cost is 35–45%**, **material cost is 40–50%**, and **overhead/miscellaneous is 10–15%** of total project cost. This ratio differs significantly from fibreglass or steel vessel construction where material dominates.

### 1.3.1 Cost Categories

1. **Direct Costs (Variable):**
   - Timber, fastenings, paint, caulking compounds
   - Direct labour (carpenters, caulkers, painters)
   - Subcontracted works (outsourced parts)

2. **Indirect Costs (Fixed/Overhead):**
   - Yard rent/lease
   - Depreciation of machinery
   - Utilities (electricity, water)
   - Insurance, permits, taxes
   - Administrative staff

3. **Contingency Reserve:** Typically **8–12%** of direct + indirect costs to absorb price fluctuations of timber and unforeseen rework.

---

## 1.4 Project Management — Formal Definition

**Project Management (PM)** in boat building is the application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet defined **scope, schedule, budget, and quality** requirements. It involves the **five process groups** — Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, and Closing.

### 1.4.1 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Project management in boat building is like **conducting an orchestra**. The project manager is the **conductor**, the carpenters, caulkers, and machinists are the **musicians**, the *Bill of Materials* is the **sheet music**, the *Gantt Chart* is the **tempo**, and the *launching day* is the **final concert**. Without coordination, even the most skilled musicians (workers) produce noise instead of music (a seaworthy boat).

### 1.4.2 The PM Triple Constraint

$$
\text{Project Success} = f(\text{Scope}, \text{Schedule}, \text{Cost}) \quad \text{with Quality in the centre}
$$

---

## 1.5 Outsourcing Parts — Formal Definition

**Outsourcing** is the strategic decision to procure specific components, sub-assemblies, or services from external suppliers/vendors rather than producing them in-house. In traditional boat building, this often includes **propellers, shaft assemblies, engine installations, navigation equipment, marine hardware, and pre-fabricated bulkheads**.

### 1.5.1 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Outsourcing a boat part is like a **tailor buying buttons from a button-seller** instead of carving each button from scratch. The tailor (boat builder) focuses on his core skill (hull carpentry), while a specialist makes a better, cheaper button (component). The decision to *make or buy* is the essence of outsourcing.

---

## 1.6 Buy or Lease of Machinery and Tools — Formal Definition

This is a **capital investment decision** comparing the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of purchasing equipment versus the periodic cost of leasing it, considering usage intensity, project duration, maintenance burden, and obsolescence risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome:** Students must demonstrate the ability to compute the **break-even usage hours** at which buying becomes more economical than leasing.

### 1.6.1 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Buying a machine is like **buying a house** (large upfront cost, long-term ownership, maintenance is your problem). Leasing a machine is like **renting an apartment** (smaller periodic payments, no maintenance burden, freedom to upgrade). The right choice depends on *how long you intend to stay* (project duration) and *how heavily you'll use it* (utilization hours).

---

## 1.7 Bill of Materials (BOM) and Detailed Costs — Formal Definition

The **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a comprehensive, structured, hierarchical list of all raw materials, sub-components, parts, fasteners, consumables, and their **quantities** required to manufacture one complete boat. The **Detailed Cost Sheet** extends the BOM with **unit rates, total costs, labour charges, and overhead allocations** to arrive at the final **landed cost** of the vessel.

### 1.7.1 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** The BOM is the **shopping list** with exact quantities (e.g., "2 kg tea, 1 litre milk"). The Detailed Cost Sheet is the **itemized grocery bill** with prices (e.g., "Tea ₹600, Milk ₹60, Total ₹660 + GST ₹39.60"). Without the BOM, you don't know *what to buy*; without the cost sheet, you don't know *what you spent*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PENST864 Module 2):** The integration of BOM → Cost Estimation → Budget → Project Schedule is the **complete financial-engineering backbone** of any traditional boat-building venture and is a high-weightage area in University examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Yard Selection — Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis

Yard selection is a **multi-criteria decision problem** that can be evaluated using the **Weighted Scoring Model**.

### 2.1.1 Weighted Scoring Model Steps
1. Identify decision criteria (e.g., proximity to water, accessibility, labour, utilities, cost).
2. Assign a **weight** $w_i$ to each criterion based on importance ($\sum w_i = 1.0$).
3. Score each candidate yard on a scale of **1 to 10** for each criterion.
4. Compute the **Weighted Score** for each yard.
5. Select the yard with the **highest weighted score**.

$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $S_j$ = Total weighted score of yard *j*
- $w_i$ = Weight of criterion *i*
- $r_{ij}$ = Rating of yard *j* on criterion *i*
- $n$ = Number of criteria

### 2.1.2 Why This Approach?
- It is **objective** (numerical output).
- It accommodates **qualitative factors** (labour skill, climate).
- It is **transparent** — stakeholders can debate weights, not just outcomes.

---

## 2.2 Workshop/Yard Requirements — Engineering Sizing Principles

### 2.2.1 Slipway Length Calculation

$$
L_{\text{slipway}} = L_{\text{vessel}} + L_{\text{cradle}} + L_{\text{launching margin}}
$$

Typically:
- $L_{\text{cradle}} \approx 0.6 \times L_{\text{vessel}}$
- $L_{\text{launching margin}} \approx 5$ to $8$ **m**

**Example:** For a 20 m vessel → Slipway = $20 + 12 + 7 = 39$ **m**.

### 2.2.2 Timber Storage Sizing (Air Seasoning)

$$
V_{\text{storage}} = \frac{M_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{seasoning}}}{\rho \times S}
$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{total}}$ = Total timber mass required (kg)
- $T_{\text{seasoning}}$ = Seasoning period (typically **6–18 months** for teak)
- $\rho$ = Wood density (teak ≈ **660 kg/m³**)
- $S$ = Stacking efficiency factor (≈ **0.55** for stickered stacks)

### 2.2.3 Crane/Gantry Capacity
$$
C_{\text{crane}} \geq 1.25 \times W_{\text{vessel (lightweight)}}
$$

A **25% safety margin** is mandated by **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** and most classification societies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.25×?** Because the lifting operation involves dynamic shock loads (wind gusts, sudden starts) that can transiently exceed the static weight by 20–30%. The factor of safety accounts for this.

---

## 2.3 Budgeting — Cost Estimation Models

### 2.3.1 Top-Down (Analogous) Estimating

$$
C_{\text{new project}} = C_{\text{similar past project}} \times \frac{S_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{old}}} \times \text{Index Factor}
$$

### 2.3.2 Bottom-Up (Parametric) Estimating

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} (Q_k \times P_k) + C_{\text{labour}} + C_{\text{overhead}} + C_{\text{contingency}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_k$ = Quantity of item *k*
- $P_k$ = Unit price of item *k*
- $m$ = Number of line-items
- $C_{\text{contingency}} = 0.10 \times (C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{labour}})$

### 2.3.3 Learning Curve Effect (for repeat production)

$$
C_n = C_1 \times n^{b}
$$

Where:
- $C_n$ = Cost of the *n*-th unit
- $C_1$ = Cost of the first unit
- $b = \log_2(\text{learning rate})$
- For an **80% learning curve**, $b = \log_2(0.80) \approx -0.3219$

---

## 2.4 Project Management — Scheduling Models

### 2.4.1 Critical Path Method (CPM)
The CPM identifies the **longest path** of dependent activities, determining the **minimum project duration**.

$$
T_{\text{project}} = \max_{p \in \text{paths}} \sum_{i \in p} t_i
$$

Where $t_i$ is the duration of activity *i* on path *p*.

### 2.4.2 Earned Value Management (EVM)

$$
\text{CV (Cost Variance)} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}
$$

$$
\text{SV (Schedule Variance)} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}
$$

$$
\text{CPI (Cost Performance Index)} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}
$$

$$
\text{SPI (Schedule Performance Index)} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rules:**
> - CPI > 1 → Under budget (good)
> - CPI < 1 → Over budget (bad)
> - SPI > 1 → Ahead of schedule (good)
> - SPI < 1 → Behind schedule (bad)

### 2.4.3 Gantt Chart
A Gantt chart is a **bar-chart representation** of tasks against time, used to visualize project schedule, dependencies (via arrows), milestones, and critical path.

---

## 2.5 Outsourcing — Make-or-Buy Decision Framework

### 2.5.1 Cost-Based Make-or-Buy

$$
\text{Buy if:} \quad C_{\text{in-house}} > C_{\text{outsourced}} + C_{\text{coordination}}
$$

$$
\text{Buy if:} \quad C_{\text{in-house}} > C_{\text{outsourced}} + C_{\text{quality risk}}
$$

### 2.5.2 Strategic Make-or-Buy
| Factor | Make In-House | Outsource (Buy) |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Core competency** | Yes (hull carpentry) | No (engine install) |
| **Volume needed** | High | Low |
| **Specialized skill** | Available | Scarce |
| **Confidentiality** | High | Low |
| **Capacity available** | Yes | No |

---

## 2.6 Buy or Lease — Capital Recovery Analysis

### 2.6.1 Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) Method

$$
\text{EUAC}_{\text{buy}} = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + C_{\text{maintenance}} - S \cdot (A/F, i, n)
$$

$$
\text{EUAC}_{\text{lease}} = L \cdot (1 + t)
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Purchase price
- $L$ = Annual lease rental
- $C_{\text{maintenance}}$ = Annual maintenance cost
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of year *n*
- $i$ = Interest/discount rate
- $t$ = Tax rate (if applicable)
- $(A/P, i, n)$ = Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$
- $(A/F, i, n)$ = Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** Choose the option with the **lower EUAC**.

### 2.6.2 Break-Even Usage Hours

$$
H_{\text{break-even}} = \frac{P - S}{L_{\text{hourly}} - C_{\text{maint. hourly}}}
$$

Where $L_{\text{hourly}}$ is the equivalent hourly leasing cost and $C_{\text{maint. hourly}}$ is the hourly maintenance cost of the owned machine.

> If expected annual usage $H > H_{\text{break-even}}$ → **Buy**.
> If expected annual usage $H < H_{\text{break-even}}$ → **Lease**.

---

## 2.7 Bill of Materials & Detailed Cost Sheet — Structure

### 2.7.1 BOM Hierarchy (Boat-Building Specific)
- **Level 0:** Complete Vessel (e.g., 18 m traditional fishing trawler)
- **Level 1:** Major assemblies (Hull, Superstructure, Deck, Engine, Electrical, Outfitting)
- **Level 2:** Sub-assemblies (Hull frames, Hull planking, Keel, Stem, Sternpost)
- **Level 3:** Parts (individual planks, fasteners, knees, floors)
- **Level 4:** Raw materials (logs, sawn timber, copper nails, cotton caulking)

### 2.7.2 Cost Sheet Format (KTU-Standard)

| Line | Item | Qty | Unit | Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) | Source/Supplier |
|------|------|-----|------|----------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Teak logs (Grade I) | 8 | m³ | 85,000 | 680,000 | Local |
| 2 | Anjili wood | 3 | m³ | 42,000 | 126,000 | Local |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### 2.7.3 Marked-Up Final Cost

$$
C_{\text{final}} = C_{\text{BOM}} \times (1 + m_{\text{labour}}) \times (1 + m_{\text{overhead}}) \times (1 + c_{\text{contingency}})
$$

Where $m_{\text{labour}}$, $m_{\text{overhead}}$ are markup percentages and $c_{\text{contingency}}$ is the contingency fraction.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Yard weighted score | dimensionless |
| 2 | $L_{\text{slipway}} = L_v + 0.6L_v + L_{\text{margin}}$ | Slipway length | m |
| 3 | $C_{\text{crane}} \geq 1.25 \times W_v$ | Crane capacity | tonnes |
| 4 | $C_{\text{total}} = \sum (Q_k \times P_k) + C_L + C_O + C_C$ | Total project cost | ₹ |
| 5 | $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{b}$ | Learning curve | ₹ |
| 6 | $T_{\text{project}} = \max_p \sum t_i$ | CPM duration | days |
| 7 | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | Cost variance | ₹ |
| 8 | $\text{SPI} = \text{EV}/\text{PV}$ | Schedule performance | index |
| 9 | $\text{EUAC}_{\text{buy}} = P(A/P,i,n) + C_m - S(A/F,i,n)$ | Annualized buy cost | ₹/yr |
| 10 | $\text{EUAC}_{\text{lease}} = L(1+t)$ | Annual lease cost | ₹/yr |
| 11 | $H_{be} = (P - S)/(L_h - C_{mh})$ | Break-even hours | hr/yr |
| 12 | $C_f = C_{BOM}(1+m_L)(1+m_O)(1+c_C)$ | Final cost | ₹ |
| 13 | $(A/P,i,n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Capital recovery factor | 1/yr |
| 14 | $(A/F,i,n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Sinking fund factor | 1/yr |
| 15 | $V_{\text{storage}} = \dfrac{M \cdot T}{\rho \cdot S}$ | Timber storage volume | m³ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Yard Selection Using Weighted Scoring Model

> **Scenario:** A traditional boat builder (Kerala-based) is selecting between **3 candidate yards** (Yard A — Kochi backwater, Yard B — Beypore coastal, Yard C — Azhikode riverside) for constructing a 20 m wooden fishing vessel. Five criteria are evaluated.

**Step 1: Define criteria and weights**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ |
|-----------|---------------|
| Proximity to water (launching) | 0.30 |
| Skilled labour availability | 0.25 |
| Cost of land/lease | 0.20 |
| Transport access (road) | 0.15 |
| Climate shelter (workshop) | 0.10 |
| **Total** | **1.00** |

**Step 2: Rate each yard (scale 1–10)**

| Criterion | Weight | Yard A (Kochi) | Yard B (Beypore) | Yard C (Azhikode) |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Water proximity | 0.30 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Skilled labour | 0.25 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost of lease | 0.20 | 5 | 7 | 9 |
| Transport access | 0.15 | 9 | 6 | 7 |
| Climate shelter | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 6 |

**Step 3: Compute weighted scores**

For Yard A (Kochi):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
S_A &= 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.00 + 1.35 + 0.70 \\
S_A &= 7.75
\end{aligned}
$$

For Yard B (Beypore):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
S_B &= 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 \\
S_B &= 7.75
\end{aligned}
$$

For Yard C (Azhikode):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
S_C &= 2.10 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.60 \\
S_C &= 7.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Yards A and B are tied at **7.75**. In a tie-breaker, secondary criteria (e.g., *historical craftsmanship reputation* of Beypore in *uru* building) would favour Yard B. Alternatively, sensitivity analysis (changing weights ±10%) is performed.

**Sensitivity check for Yard A vs B:**
- If water-proximity weight increases from 0.30 → 0.40 (decrease labour to 0.15): $S_A = 9(0.40) + 8(0.15) + 5(0.20) + 9(0.15) + 7(0.10) = 3.60 + 1.20 + 1.00 + 1.35 + 0.70 = 7.85$ and $S_B = 8(0.40) + 9(0.15) + 7(0.20) + 6(0.15) + 8(0.10) = 3.20 + 1.35 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 7.65$. Now Yard A wins.
- If labour weight increases from 0.25 → 0.35: $S_A = 9(0.30) + 8(0.35) + 5(0.20) + 9(0.15) + 7(0.10) = 2.70 + 2.80 + 1.00 + 1.35 + 0.70 = 8.55$ and $S_B = 8(0.30) + 9(0.35) + 7(0.20) + 6(0.15) + 8(0.10) = 2.40 + 3.15 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 8.65$. Yard B wins.

**Conclusion:** If labour skill is the priority (which is critical in *traditional* boat building), choose **Yard B (Beypore)**. If water proximity and infrastructure dominate, choose **Yard A (Kochi)**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Slipway and Crane Sizing for 20 m Vessel

**Given:**
- Vessel Length Overall (LOA), $L_v = 20$ **m**
- Vessel Lightweight (hull mass), $W_v = 28$ **tonnes**
- Launching margin, $L_m = 7$ **m**

**Step 1: Slipway length**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{slipway}} &= L_v + (0.6 \times L_v) + L_m \\
L_{\text{slipway}} &= 20 + (0.6 \times 20) + 7 \\
L_{\text{slipway}} &= 20 + 12 + 7 \\
L_{\text{slipway}} &= 39 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Crane capacity**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{crane}} &\geq 1.25 \times W_v \\
C_{\text{crane}} &\geq 1.25 \times 28 \\
C_{\text{crane}} &\geq 35 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **40-tonne gantry crane** (next standard size) should be procured/leased for this yard.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Buy vs Lease of a Planer Machine

**Given:**
- Purchase price $P = ₹ 8,00,000$
- Useful life $n = 8$ years
- Salvage value $S = ₹ 1,00,000$
- Annual maintenance $C_m = ₹ 40,000$
- Annual lease rental $L = ₹ 1,50,000$
- Tax rate $t = 0$ (assume for simplicity)
- Discount rate $i = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 1: Compute Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, 10%, 8)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/P, 0.10, 8) &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^8}{(1.10)^8 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.10 \times 2.1436}{2.1436 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.21436}{1.1436} \\
&= 0.18744
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Sinking Fund Factor (A/F, 10%, 8)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F, 0.10, 8) &= \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^8 - 1} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{1.1436} \\
&= 0.08744
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: EUAC of Buying**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EUAC}_{\text{buy}} &= P \cdot (A/P,i,n) + C_m - S \cdot (A/F,i,n) \\
&= 8,00,000 \times 0.18744 + 40,000 - 1,00,000 \times 0.08744 \\
&= 1,49,952 + 40,000 - 8,744 \\
&= ₹ 1,81,208 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: EUAC of Leasing**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EUAC}_{\text{lease}} &= L \times (1 + t) \\
&= 1,50,000 \times (1 + 0) \\
&= ₹ 1,50,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision**
$$
\text{EUAC}_{\text{lease}} < \text{EUAC}_{\text{buy}}
$$
$$
1,50,000 < 1,81,208
$$

**Conclusion:** **Leasing is more economical by ₹ 31,208/year** in this case. The yard should lease the planer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity check:** If the planer is used for **3 shifts/day** (high utilization), the maintenance cost in the buy option effectively drops per produced unit, making buying more attractive. The student's answer should always state the assumption about utilization intensity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Build a Bill of Materials (BOM) and Cost Sheet

> **Scenario:** Constructing a **15 m traditional wooden fishing boat** (canoe-style, *vallam* type) at a Kerala yard.

**Step 1: Determine hull planking requirement**
- Hull surface area (curved) $A_h \approx 1.4 \times L \times B = 1.4 \times 15 \times 3.2 = 67.2$ **m²**
- Plank thickness $t = 25$ **mm** = 0.025 m
- Plank volume $V_p = A_h \times t = 67.2 \times 0.025 = 1.68$ **m³**
- With 30% waste and overlap: $V_{\text{timber}} = 1.68 \times 1.30 = 2.184$ **m³** → **~ 2.2 m³ teak**

**Step 2: Tabulate BOM**

| Sl. | Item | Qty | Unit | Unit Rate (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|-----|------|-----|------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | Teak logs (sawn) | 2.2 | m³ | 85,000 | 1,87,000 |
| 2 | Anjili timber (frames) | 0.8 | m³ | 42,000 | 33,600 |
| 3 | Copper nails (75 mm) | 12 | kg | 950 | 11,400 |
| 4 | Cotton caulking | 25 | kg | 220 | 5,500 |
| 5 | Marine plywood (12 mm) | 8 | sheet | 2,800 | 22,400 |
| 6 | Marine paint (antifouling) | 30 | L | 750 | 22,500 |
| 7 | Wood preservative | 15 | L | 380 | 5,700 |
| 8 | Iron keel (cast) | 1.5 | tonne | 65,000/t | 97,500 |
| | **Material Subtotal ($C_M$)** | | | | **₹ 3,85,600** |

**Step 3: Labour cost ($C_L$)** — Assume 60 man-days × 4 carpenters × 6 months @ ₹ 800/day
$$
C_L = 60 \times 4 \times 800 = ₹ 1,92,000
$$

Wait, re-evaluate: For 6 months (180 days) with 4 carpenters working most days:
$$
C_L = 180 \times 4 \times 800 = ₹ 5,76,000
$$

Let us use $C_L = ₹ 5,76,000$ as the realistic figure.

**Step 4: Overhead ($C_O$)** — Yard rental, electricity, permits ≈ 12% of $(C_M + C_L)$
$$
C_O = 0.12 \times (3,85,600 + 5,76,000) = 0.12 \times 9,61,600 = ₹ 1,15,392
$$

**Step 5: Contingency ($C_C$)** — 10% of direct cost
$$
C_C = 0.10 \times (C_M + C_L) = 0.10 \times 9,61,600 = ₹ 96,160
$$

**Step 6: Total project cost**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_M + C_L + C_O + C_C \\
&= 3,85,600 + 5,76,000 + 1,15,392 + 96,160 \\
&= ₹ 11,73,152
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Profit margin and selling price** (assume 15% profit)
$$
C_{\text{sell}} = C_{\text{total}} \times 1.15 = ₹ 13,49,125
$$

**Step 8: Cost per metre LOA**
$$
C_{\text{per m}} = \frac{11,73,152}{15} = ₹ 78,210 \text{ per metre}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Kerala traditional *vallams* typically cost **₹ 60,000–90,000 per metre LOA** (2024 market rates), validating this estimate.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Earned Value Management at Project Mid-Point

**Given (a boat-building project of 200 days, ₹ 12,00,000 budget):**
- At day **100** (50% schedule progress):
  - Planned Value, $PV = 0.50 \times 12,00,000 = ₹ 6,00,000$
  - Earned Value, $EV = 0.45 \times 12,00,000 = ₹ 5,40,000$ (only 45% work actually complete)
  - Actual Cost, $AC = ₹ 5,80,000$ (spent more than earned)

**Compute variances and indices:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 5,40,000 - 5,80,000 = -₹ 40,000 \\
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 5,40,000 - 6,00,000 = -₹ 60,000 \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{5,40,000}{5,80,000} = 0.931 \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{5,40,000}{6,00,000} = 0.900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- CPI = 0.931 < 1 → **Over budget** (for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.931 of work was earned).
- SPI = 0.900 < 1 → **Behind schedule** (only 90% of planned work has been completed in 50% of the time).

**Estimate at Completion (EAC):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{12,00,000}{0.931} = ₹ 12,89,043
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The project is **expected to overshoot budget by ₹ 89,043** if corrective action is not taken. The project manager should investigate cost overruns (e.g., timber price hike, rework) and schedule slippage (e.g., delayed timber seasoning).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Make-or-Buy Decision (Propeller Shaft Assembly)

**Given:**
- In-house production cost per shaft = ₹ 1,20,000
- Outsourced purchase cost per shaft = ₹ 95,000
- Coordination/inspection cost if outsourced = ₹ 8,000 per unit
- Capacity currently available in-house: 25% idle
- Core competency test: Shaft machining is **not** a core skill of this traditional yard.

**Decision analysis:**
$$
C_{\text{in-house}} = ₹ 1,20,000
$$
$$
C_{\text{outsourced, total}} = 95,000 + 8,000 = ₹ 1,03,000
$$

Since $1,03,000 < 1,20,000$ and shaft machining is non-core → **Outsource**.

Additionally, this frees up 25% idle capacity for **hull carpentry work** that is the yard's actual strength.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Decision Domain | Key Method | Critical Input | Output | Sensitivity |
|------------------|------------|----------------|--------|-------------|
| Yard Selection | Weighted Scoring | Criteria weights, ratings | Best yard | High to weights |
| Yard Sizing | Geometric formula | Vessel LOA, beam | Area/m length | Low |
| Cost Estimation | Parametric | Qty, unit rates | Total cost | High to timber price |
| Scheduling | CPM, Gantt | Activity duration | Project duration | Medium |
| EVM | Variance analysis | EV, PV, AC | CPI, SPI | High |
| Make/Buy | Cost comparison | In-house vs vendor cost | Buy/Make | Medium |
| Buy/Lease | EUAC | P, S, L, i, n | EUAC | High to utilization |
| BOM | Hierarchical listing | Drawings, spec | Quantity list | Low |
