# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Internal Layout" with respect to traditional boat building. List any four components that constitute the internal layout.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> *Internal layout refers to the systematic arrangement and structural organization of all components fitted inside the hull of a traditional vessel — including frames, keelson, stringers, thwarts, flooring, mast step, bulkheads, and decks — to ensure structural integrity, seaworthiness, and functional usability.*

**Four Components (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Frames (ribs)** — Transverse structural members maintaining hull shape.
2. **Keelson** — Longitudinal inner backbone running above the keel.
3. **Thwarts** — Transverse seats for rowers/passengers.
4. **Limber boards** — Bottom planks with drainage holes to channel bilge water.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the function of limber holes in a traditional boat's bilge system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> *Limber holes are circular or rectangular openings cut into the **limber boards** at the lowest point of the bilge. Their function is to **allow bilge water to flow freely** along the length of the hull toward the **bilge sump** (lowest collection point), from where it can be **bailed out manually or pumped out**. Without limber holes, water would pool between frames and accelerate timber rot. The standard diameter is **25–40 mm**, spaced at intervals matching the frame spacing.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-level internal choice means students answer EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B). Both must be of equal difficulty.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the **internal layout of a traditional sailing vessel (uru/dhow)**. Label all major components and explain the function of each zone.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A traditional plank-built boat has an internal hull length of **9.6 m** and is to be fitted with **16 frames**. Determine:
- (i) The frame (station) spacing.
- (ii) The keelson thickness if the plank thickness is **32 mm**.
- (iii) The vertical position of the side stringer from the centerline if the maximum beam is **3.2 m**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Sketch (3 Marks)** — Student should draw a longitudinal section + transverse cross-section showing:
- Keel, keelson, floor frames, side stringers, sheer clamp, thwarts, mast step, partner, deck beams.

**Description (4 Marks — 1 per zone):**

| Zone | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Transverse | Frames | Maintain hull shape, resist racking |
| Longitudinal | Keelson + stringers | Distribute longitudinal loads |
| Flooring | Limber boards + holes | Drainage to bilge sump |
| Seating | Thwarts | Rowing support, hull bracing |
| Rigging | Mast step + partner | Sail support |
| Subdivision | Bulkheads + deck | Watertight integrity |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Frame Spacing [2 Marks]**

$$S_f = \dfrac{L_{hull}}{N_{frames} + 1} = \dfrac{9.6}{16 + 1} = \dfrac{9.6}{17} = 0.565 \text{ m} \approx 565 \text{ mm}$$

**Within acceptable range of 300–600 mm ✓**

**(ii) Keelson Thickness [2 Marks]**

$$t_k = 1.5 \times t_p = 1.5 \times 32 = 48 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Stringer Position [3 Marks]**

$$Y_s = \dfrac{B_{max}}{4} = \dfrac{3.2}{4} = 0.8 \text{ m from centerline}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **function and layout of frames** in a traditional boat. Discuss the difference between **floor frames, cant frames, and half frames** with reference to their position in the hull.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **bilge drainage system** of a traditional wooden boat. Why is the placement of limber holes critical to vessel longevity?

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Function of Frames (3 Marks):**
- Maintain hull cross-section shape
- Transfer loads from planks to keelson
- Resist racking (twisting) forces
- Provide attachment points for thwarts and stringers

**Types of Frames (4 Marks — 1.33 each):**

| Type | Position | Shape | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Floor Frame | Midship section | U-shaped (across bottom) | Primary load-bearing |
| Cant Frame | Bow/Stern | Diagonal/triangular | Shape tapering ends |
| Half Frame | Topsides only | L-shaped | Reinforce upper hull |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Show limber boards with holes along the bottom, water flowing toward bilge sump at lowest point.

**Explanation (4 Marks):**

- Limber boards are longitudinal planks at the bilge bottom.
- Limber holes (25–40 mm dia.) are cut at frame intersections.
- Water flows through holes toward the **bilge sump** (lowest point, usually slightly aft of midship).
- Sump is fitted with a **bailer or pump**.

**Critical Importance (1 Mark):**
> Without limber holes, water becomes trapped between frames → timber rot → structural failure within **5–7 years**. Properly limbered boats last **30–50+ years**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Internal Layout Questions:**
>
> 1. ❌ **Skipping variable definitions** in numerical answers — Examiner awards **0 marks** if you write a formula without defining symbols.
> 2. ❌ **Drawing without labels** — A sketch without component labels gets **at most 1 mark** out of 3.
> 3. ❌ **Confusing keelson with keel** — Keel is *external* (in water); Keelson is *internal* (above keel). Examiners deduct for this confusion.
> 4. ❌ **Wrong unit conversions** — Always convert m → mm or vice versa consistently.
> 5. ❌ **Omitting the "+1"** in frame spacing formula — This is the #1 calculation error in KTU scripts.
> 6. ❌ **Not relating layout to function** — Pure memorization without functional reasoning scores lower than applied reasoning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

- 📌 **Internal layout** = the complete arrangement of structural and functional components inside a hull.
- 📌 **Seven functional zones:** Hull shell, transverse framing, longitudinal reinforcement, flooring/bilge, seating/access, rigging support, deck/subdivision.
- 📌 **Frame spacing formula:** $S_f = L_{hull} / (N_{frames} + 1)$ — never forget the **+1**.
- 📌 **Keelson thickness:** $t_k = 1.5 \times t_p$ — keelson is always thicker than plank.
- 📌 **Stringer position:** at **¼ of beam** from centerline (turn of bilge).
- 📌 **Mast step** at **1/3 of LWL from bow** (fore-and-aft rig).
- 📌 **Limber holes:** 25–40 mm diameter, at every frame intersection.
- 📌 **Thwart ergonomic height:** 400–500 mm above floor.
- 📌 **Cant frames** = bow/stern; **Floor frames** = midship; **Half frames** = topsides.
- 📌 **Limber holes** prevent waterlogging and extend vessel life from years to decades.
- 📌 **Load path continuity** is the *golden rule* — break it, and the boat breaks.
- 📌 Always answer with **sketch + labels + function + calculation** in 14-mark questions.
- 📌 **Bulkheads** add watertight subdivision AND structural panel stiffness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS IN TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> *Electrical Systems* in traditional boat building refers to the integrated network of low-voltage (typically **12V DC** or **24V DC**) components, conductors, protection devices, and energy sources designed to power essential onboard functions such as navigation lighting, bilge pumping, communication, and instrument monitoring, while maintaining strict compliance with maritime safety standards (ISO 13297 / ABYC E-11).

In the context of traditional (wooden/fiberglass) boat construction, an electrical system is **not** the propulsion engine itself, but rather the **auxiliary power distribution network** that runs parallel to the hull and structural members, supplying electricity to safety-critical and convenience loads.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Boat as a Floating House"**

Imagine a traditional wooden boat as a **small floating village**. Just as a village needs a power grid (wires, a power station, fuse boxes, and street lights), a boat needs an **electrical system**. However, unlike a house connected to an infinite municipal grid, a boat is an **island of energy** — it must generate, store, and conserve every watt of electricity it uses.

Think of it this way:
- **Battery** = The village's water reservoir (stores energy)
- **Alternator / Solar Panel** = The water pump (replenishes the reservoir)
- **Wires** = The pipelines (carry the resource)
- **Fuse / Circuit Breaker** = The safety valve (prevents disasters)
- **Load (Lights, Pumps)** = The homes that consume the resource

### 1.2.1 Why Electrical Systems Matter in Traditional Boats

Traditional boat builders historically relied on **kerosene lanterns, manual pumps, and mechanical compasses**. The modern retrofitting of electrical systems into traditional hulls must:
1. Preserve the **aesthetic and structural integrity** of the wood.
2. Resist the **corrosive marine environment** (saltwater, humidity).
3. Function reliably in **vibration, heel, and pitch** conditions.
4. Operate in a **closed energy loop** (no external power source while at sea).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Voltage Metrics in Recreational/Traditional Boats:**
> - **12V DC** — Most common in small traditional boats (< 12 meters).
> - **24V DC** — Used in larger vessels for reduced current (and thus thinner cables).
> - **230V AC / 110V AC** — Only when shore power ( marina supply) is connected through an **isolation transformer**.

---

## 1.3 Primary Components of a Boat Electrical System

| S.No | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Battery (House + Starter)** | Stores DC electrical energy chemically |
| 2 | **Alternator / Outboard Generator** | Converts mechanical rotation → DC electricity |
| 3 | **Solar PV Panel + MPPT Controller** | Harvests sunlight → charges battery |
| 4 | **Bus Bar (Positive & Negative)** | Central distribution point for circuits |
| 5 | **Fuse / Circuit Breaker Panel** | Overcurrent protection |
| 6 | **Marine-Grade Tinned Copper Wires** | Conductors resistant to corrosion |
| 7 | **Navigation Lights (Port, Starboard, Stern, Masthead)** | Safety and legal compliance (COLREGS) |
| 8 | **Bilge Pump (Electric + Float Switch)** | Removes unwanted water from hull |
| 9 | **VHF Radio + GPS** | Communication and navigation electronics |
| 10 | **Isolation Transformer / Galvanic Isolator** | Protects against shore-power corrosion |

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Electrical Quantities (Recap for KTU)

For all subsequent analysis, the following three foundational quantities are mandatory:

$$V = I \times R \quad \text{(Ohm's Law — Voltage, Current, Resistance)}$$

$$P = V \times I \quad \text{(Electrical Power in Watts)}$$

$$E = P \times t \quad \text{(Electrical Energy in Watt-hours)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These three equations form the **backbone** of every boat electrical calculation. The KTU examiner expects students to apply these fluently in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of a Marine Electrical System

A traditional boat's electrical system is architecturally divided into **three distinct tiers**, each serving a unique purpose:

### 🔹 Tier 1 — Energy Generation (Source)
- **Alternator** (driven by outboard/inboard engine)
- **Solar PV panels** (with MPPT or PWM controller)
- **Wind generator** (rare in traditional small boats)
- **Shore power charger** (only at marina)

### 🔹 Tier 2 — Energy Storage (Battery Bank)
- **Lead-Acid (Flooded / AGM / Gel)** — most common, cheap, heavy
- **LiFePO₄ (Lithium Iron Phosphate)** — modern, lightweight, expensive
- Battery capacity is rated in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**

### 🔹 Tier 3 — Energy Consumption (Loads)
- Lighting, pumps, electronics, refrigeration
- Each load is protected by an individual **fuse** sized at **125%** of its full-load current (per ABYC E-11).

---

## 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — Concept Logic Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Each Tier:**

- **Why Generation?** — Boats are energy islands; they must create their own power.
- **Why Storage?** — Loads (especially navigation lights at night) must operate when the engine is OFF and sun is absent. Batteries bridge this gap.
- **Why Protection (Fuses)?** — A short circuit in a boat can cause **fire at sea**, where escape is impossible. Fuses are non-negotiable safety devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" of Current Flow:**

Electrical current in a boat follows a **single-direction (DC)** flow path:

$$
\text{Source (+)} \longrightarrow \text{Fuse} \longrightarrow \text{Switch} \longrightarrow \text{Load} \longrightarrow \text{Return (- Bus)} \longrightarrow \text{Source (-)}
$$

This is fundamentally different from household AC systems, which use **alternating current** and can share a common neutral.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE — questions are often directly numerical.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application in Boats |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $V = I \times R$ | V=Voltage, I=Current, R=Resistance | V, A, Ω | Cable sizing, load analysis |
| 2 | $P = V \times I$ | P=Power, V=Voltage, I=Current | W (Watts) | Solar panel sizing, load list |
| 3 | $E = P \times t$ | E=Energy, t=time | Wh (Watt-hours) | Battery autonomy calculations |
| 4 | $Q = I \times t$ | Q=Charge, I=Current, t=time | Ah (Ampere-hours) | Battery capacity selection |
| 5 | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \times L \times 2 \times \rho}{A}$ | Voltage drop in cable | V | Cable cross-section sizing |
| 6 | $A_{cable} = \dfrac{I \times L \times 2 \times \rho}{V_{drop,allow}}$ | Required cable area | mm² | Wire gauge selection |
| 7 | $\eta_{system} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | System efficiency | % | Solar + battery + inverter loss |
| 8 | $I_{load} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{V_{system}}$ | Current drawn by load | A | Fuse and breaker sizing |
| 9 | $t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{battery} \times V_{bat} \times DoD}{P_{total,load}}$ | Battery backup time | hours | Endurance at sea |
| 10 | $P_{panel} = \dfrac{E_{daily}}{\eta_{charge} \times H_{peak-sun}}$ | Solar panel rating | Wp | Renewable sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Constants (Memorize):**
> - Resistivity of copper: $\rho_{Cu} = 0.0172 \;\Omega \cdot mm^2 / m$ at 20°C.
> - Resistivity of tinned copper (marine): $\rho_{tCu} \approx 0.0175 \;\Omega \cdot mm^2 / m$.
> - Allowable voltage drop in marine DC circuits: **≤ 3%** for non-critical loads, **≤ 10%** for starters (per ABYC E-11).
> - Depth of Discharge (DoD) for lead-acid: **50%** (to preserve cycle life).
> - Depth of Discharge (DoD) for LiFePO₄: **80–90%** (safe).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Importance

The electrical system in a traditional boat is **mission-critical**:
- **Navigation lights** failing at night = **collision risk** (COLREGS violation).
- **Bilge pump** failing = **sinking risk**.
- **VHF radio** failing = **no distress call capability**.
- **Corrosion** from improper grounding = **metal fastener degradation** in wooden hulls.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic is also a bridge to the **Ocean Engineering** and **Renewable Energy** electives, where students may later design **solar-electric hybrid traditional fishing vessels** (a Kerala-state-promoted initiative).

> [!NOTE]
> **Did you know?**
> The Kerala State Government has funded the conversion of diesel-powered traditional fishing boats to **solar-electric hybrid propulsion** as part of the *Suchitwa Mission*. Engineers trained in traditional boat electrical systems are directly employable in this green-transition sector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cable Cross-Section Sizing (Voltage Drop Method)

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU for boat electrical topics. We derive the formula for selecting the correct wire thickness so that voltage drop stays within marine standards.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Ohm's Law

Ohm's law states that voltage drop across a conductor is:

$$V_{drop} = I \times R_{cable}$$

### Step 2: Express Resistance in Terms of Geometry

The resistance of a uniform conductor is given by:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity of conductor material ($\Omega \cdot mm^2/m$ for copper)
- $L$ = one-way length of the cable (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the conductor ($mm^2$)

### Step 3: Account for Round-Trip (Return Path)

In a DC boat circuit, current must travel **out** through the positive wire AND **return** through the negative wire. Therefore, the **total conductor length** in the circuit is:

$$L_{total} = 2 \times L$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Total Length

Substituting the round-trip length into the resistance equation:

$$R_{cable} = \dfrac{\rho \times 2L}{A}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into Ohm's Law

$$V_{drop} = I \times \dfrac{\rho \times 2L}{A}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to Solve for Cross-Sectional Area $A$

Rearranging algebraically to isolate $A$:

$$A = \dfrac{I \times 2L \times \rho}{V_{drop}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical KTU formula** — students should be able to write it from memory and apply it to a numerical problem in under 2 minutes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Navigation Light Cable Sizing

> **Problem Statement:**
> A traditional wooden boat has a **port-side navigation light** located **8 m** from the battery. The light consumes **0.5 A** at **12 V DC**. Using tinned copper cable ($\rho = 0.0175 \;\Omega \cdot mm^2/m$) and an allowable voltage drop of **3%**, calculate the required cable cross-section.

**Given Data:**
- $I = 0.5\;\text{A}$
- $L = 8\;\text{m}$ (one-way)
- $V_{system} = 12\;\text{V}$
- $V_{drop,allow} = 3\% \times 12\;\text{V} = 0.36\;\text{V}$
- $\rho = 0.0175\;\Omega \cdot mm^2/m$

**Step 1 — Identify the Formula:**

$$A = \dfrac{I \times 2L \times \rho}{V_{drop}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$A = \dfrac{0.5 \times 2 \times 8 \times 0.0175}{0.36}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator:**

$$0.5 \times 2 = 1.0$$
$$1.0 \times 8 = 8.0$$
$$8.0 \times 0.0175 = 0.14$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Denominator:**

$$A = \dfrac{0.14}{0.36} = 0.389\;\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Safety Margin & Standard Sizing:**

Round up to the **nearest standard size** available in marine catalogues. Standard tinned copper sizes are: **0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10.0 mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected cable size: $A_{selected} = 0.75\;\text{mm}^2$** (the next standard size above 0.389 mm², providing a 1.93× safety margin).

**Step 6 — Verify the Actual Voltage Drop with Selected Cable:**

$$V_{drop,actual} = \dfrac{I \times 2L \times \rho}{A_{selected}}$$

$$V_{drop,actual} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 2 \times 8 \times 0.0175}{0.75} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.75} = 0.1867\;\text{V}$$

**Step 7 — Verify Percentage Drop:**

$$\%V_{drop} = \dfrac{0.1867}{12} \times 100 = 1.56\%$$

Since $1.56\% < 3\%$, the selection is **valid**. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Battery Backup Time (Autonomy Calculation)

### Step 1: Battery Capacity Definition

A battery rated at $C$ Ah can theoretically deliver $C$ amperes for 1 hour, or 1 ampere for $C$ hours.

$$Q = I \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{Q}{I}$$

### Step 2: Account for Usable Depth of Discharge (DoD)

A lead-acid battery should never be fully discharged — it permanently damages the plates. We therefore use only a fraction of its rated capacity:

$$Q_{usable} = C \times DoD$$

where:
- $DoD_{Lead-Acid} = 0.50$ (50%)
- $DoD_{LiFePO_4} = 0.85$ (85%)

### Step 3: Include System Inefficiency

Real systems lose energy to inverter, cable heating, and charge controller inefficiencies (typically 10–20%):

$$\eta_{system} \approx 0.85$$

### Step 4: Final Backup Time Formula

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{battery} \times V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{system}}{P_{total,load}}$$

Or equivalently, using current directly:

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{battery} \times DoD \times \eta_{system}}{I_{total,load}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Battery Autonomy for Night Sailing

> **Problem Statement:**
> A traditional boat has the following night-time DC loads: navigation lights (3 × 10W = 30W), VHF radio (5W standby + 25W receive = 30W), cabin light (20W), and bilge pump intermittent (60W, runs 5% of time = 3W effective). The battery is a **12V, 100Ah lead-acid** unit. Calculate the backup time.

**Step 1 — Total Continuous Load:**

$$P_{total} = 30 + 30 + 20 + 3 = 83\;\text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Current:**

$$I_{total} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{V_{system}} = \dfrac{83}{12} = 6.92\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply DoD and Efficiency:**

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C \times DoD \times \eta}{I_{total}}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.50 \times 0.85}{6.92}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{42.5}{6.92} = 6.14\;\text{hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: Approximately 6.1 hours of night-time operation** before the battery must be recharged. This is the KTU-typical autonomy figure for a small traditional fishing boat.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Fuse Sizing Rules (ABYC E-11 Compliant)

| Load Type | Continuous Current ($I_{cont}$) | Fuse Rating Rule | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Navigation lights | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | Safety margin against inrush |
| Bilge pump | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | Motor starting surge |
| VHF Radio | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{cont}$ | TX mode draws more |
| Solar panel (string) | $I_{sc}$ (from panel) | $1.56 \times I_{sc}$ | Accounts for irradiance > STC |
| Inverter input | $P/V$ | $1.25 \times I_{rated}$ | Surge capacity buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 125% rule is a KTU board favorite** — if a 10A load is protected, the fuse must be $1.25 \times 10 = 12.5\;\text{A}$. Round up to the next standard fuse: **15 A**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Boat Electrical Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a traditional boat's electrical system, from energy generation through storage, distribution, protection, and consumption.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Sources] --> B[Charge Controller / Regulator]
    B --> C[Battery Bank 12V DC]
    C --> D[Main Fuse 80A]
    D --> E[Positive Bus Bar]
    E --> F1[Fuse 10A Nav Lights]
    E --> F2[Fuse 5A VHF Radio]
    E --> F3[Fuse 8A Bilge Pump]
    E --> F4[Fuse 3A Cabin Lights]
    F1 --> L1[Port Light Red]
    F1 --> L2[Starboard Light Green]
    F1 --> L3[Stern Light White]
    F1 --> L4[Masthead Light White]
    F2 --> R1[VHF Radio Transceiver]
    F3 --> P1[Bilge Pump 12V DC]
    F4 --> L5[Cabin LED Lights]
    C --> N[Negative Bus Bar]
    N --> G1[L1]
    N --> G2[L2]
    N --> G3[L3]
    N --> G4[L4]
    N --> G5[R1]
    N --> G6[P1]
    N --> G7[L5]
    H[Shore Power 230V AC] --> I[Isolation Transformer]
    I --> J[Galvanic Isolator]
    J --> K[Battery Charger AC-DC]
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Energy flows from sources (top) → storage (battery) → main fuse → bus bar → individual fused branches → loads. The negative return path collects at the negative bus bar, which is bonded to the battery's negative terminal. Shore power enters through a safety chain of **Isolation Transformer → Galvanic Isolator → Charger** to prevent hull corrosion.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Single Branch Circuit (Navigation Light)

This is the most common sub-circuit found in any KTU exam asking to "draw and explain a typical boat lighting circuit."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Positive +12V] --> B[Main Fuse 1A]
    B --> C[Switch SPST]
    C --> D[Navigation Light Bulb LED]
    D --> E[Battery Negative 0V Ground]
    E --> A
```

**Explanation of Each Element:**
- **Battery (+12V)** — DC source
- **Main Fuse (1A)** — protects the wire from short circuit
- **SPST Switch** — single-pole single-throw manual control
- **LED Light** — the load (typically 1–3W)
- **Return to (−)** — completes the circuit

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Solar Charging Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sunlight Hits PV Panel] --> S2[Panel Produces DC Electricity]
    S2 --> S3{MPPT Controller Active?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Tracks Maximum Power Point]
    S3 -- No --> S5[PWM Charging Mode]
    S4 --> S6[Regulated DC to Battery]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Battery State of Charge Increasing]
    S7 --> S8{Battery Full?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Controller Switches to Float Mode]
    S8 -- No --> S6
    S9 --> S10[Maintains 13.6V Trickle]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPPT vs. PWM (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **MPPT** (Maximum Power Point Tracking) extracts **20–30% more energy** by dynamically adjusting the panel's operating voltage. It is **expensive** but **efficient**.
> - **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation) is **cheaper** but simply connects the panel directly to the battery, wasting excess voltage as heat.
> - For traditional boat retrofits in Kerala's tropical sun, **MPPT is strongly recommended**.

---

## 4.4 Wiring Topology — Backbone vs. Branch (Decision Matrix)

| Topology | Description | Best Use Case in Boats | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Radial (Tree)** | Each load runs its own cable from the bus | Most common; clean fault isolation | Standard answer for KTU diagrams |
| **Ring (Loop)** | Cables form a closed loop; two paths to each load | High-reliability vessels (yachts) | Advanced topic |
| **Combined** | Mix of both | Modern traditional boat builds | Briefly mention in exams |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a "Bus Bar" in the context of a boat's electrical system. Why is it preferred over twisting multiple wires together at a single point?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

A **bus bar** is a solid metallic conductor (typically tinned copper) that serves as a **common collection point** for multiple circuits. In a boat, there are two bus bars: a **positive bus bar** (fed from the battery via the main fuse) and a **negative bus bar** (connected to battery negative / common ground).

**Why preferred over twisted connections:**
1. **Lower contact resistance** — a solid bar minimizes voltage drop.
2. **Mechanical stability** — withstands vibration inherent in marine environments.
3. **Easier maintenance** — individual circuits can be added/removed without disturbing others.
4. **Reduced fire risk** — eliminates loose strands that could cause short circuits.
5. **Neat installation** — improves traceability during fault diagnosis.

*(3 marks — definition 1, advantages 2)*

---

### **Q2. What is "galvanic corrosion" and how does a galvanic isolator protect a traditional boat's metal fittings?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

**Galvanic corrosion** is the electrochemical destruction of metals when two dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller and steel hull fitting) are electrically connected in the presence of an **electrolyte** (seawater). The more *active* metal (anode) corrodes preferentially.

When a boat is connected to **shore power**, a small stray DC current can flow through the grounding wire, accelerating corrosion of underwater metal fittings.

A **galvanic isolator** is a device installed in the shore-power grounding wire that **blocks DC currents below ~1.4V** (using semiconductor diodes) while still allowing **AC fault currents** to pass for safety. This prevents the destructive DC galvanic loop while preserving protection against electric shock.

*(3 marks — phenomenon 1, mechanism 1, protection 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the **factor of 2 (round-trip length)** in voltage-drop calculations. This alone costs **3 of 14 marks** in typical ESE papers.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using **power in kW but voltage in V inconsistently** — always keep units uniform.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not stating the **standard (ABYC / ISO)** when justifying cable size or fuse rating. Examiners reward students who cite a code.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing bus bars as **single lines** instead of showing **positive AND negative** rails. Both must appear.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **MPPT with PWM** — explain the efficiency gain (~20–30%) explicitly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Solar-Electrical Design for a Traditional Fishing Boat**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a 12V DC electrical system installed in a traditional wooden fishing boat. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traditional fishing boat is equipped with the following DC loads operating in daylight: a **30W VHF radio**, a **15W GPS**, a **20W fish-finder**, and a **10W cabin fan**, all at **12V DC**. The boat has one **100 Wp solar panel** with an MPPT charge controller ($\eta = 0.92$) and a **12V, 100Ah lead-acid battery** (DoD = 50%). Assuming **5 peak sun hours/day**, calculate: **(i)** the daily energy consumption, **(ii)** the daily solar energy harvested, and **(iii)** the battery backup time if all loads run continuously. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram (5 marks) and Component List (2 marks):**

The block diagram should mirror the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** above. The student must include:

1. **Solar PV Panel (100 Wp)** — converts sunlight to DC electricity.
2. **MPPT Charge Controller** — regulates panel output, prevents battery overcharge, maximizes harvested energy.
3. **Battery Bank (12V, 100Ah Lead-Acid)** — stores energy for night use and engine-off operation.
4. **Main Fuse (80A)** — protects the entire distribution system from catastrophic short circuit.
5. **Positive & Negative Bus Bars** — central distribution terminals.
6. **Individual Branch Fuses (3A, 5A, 8A, 10A)** — per ABYC E-11, each load fused at 125% of full-load current.
7. **Loads** — VHF radio, GPS, fish-finder, cabin fan, navigation lights, bilge pump.
8. **Negative Return Path** — completes the DC circuit back to the battery's negative terminal.

*(Full 7 marks: 4 for diagram completeness, 1 for correct symbols/polarity, 2 for component list with function.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Daily Energy Consumption:**

The loads run during daylight, so use **5 hours of operation** (peak sun hours = typical operating time).

$$P_{total} = 30 + 15 + 20 + 10 = 75\;\text{W}$$

$$E_{daily} = P_{total} \times t = 75 \times 5 = 375\;\text{Wh/day}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step (ii) — Daily Solar Energy Harvested:**

$$E_{solar} = P_{panel} \times H_{peak-sun} \times \eta_{MPPT}$$

$$E_{solar} = 100 \times 5 \times 0.92 = 460\;\text{Wh/day}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step (iii) — Battery Backup Time (All Loads Continuous):**

First, total load current:

$$I_{total} = \dfrac{P_{total}}{V_{system}} = \dfrac{75}{12} = 6.25\;\text{A}$$

Now apply the autonomy formula:

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{C \times DoD}{I_{total}}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.50}{6.25} = \dfrac{50}{6.25} = 8.0\;\text{hours}$$

*(2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With continuous full-load operation, the battery can sustain the system for **8 hours** before requiring recharge. Since daily solar harvest (460 Wh) exceeds daily consumption (375 Wh), the boat's energy budget is **sustainable** — a surplus of 85 Wh/day is available for night loads or battery rebalancing.

**Total marks for Q(b): 7**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cable Sizing & Protection Design**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** A traditional boat has a **bilge pump** rated **12V, 60W**, located **6 m** from the battery. The pump is fitted with a **float switch** for automatic operation. Using **tinned copper cable** ($\rho = 0.0175\;\Omega\cdot mm^2/m$) and an **allowable voltage drop of 3%**, determine: **(i)** the full-load current, **(ii)** the minimum required cable cross-section, and **(iii)** the correctly rated fuse size as per ABYC E-11. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **single-line diagram**, explain the working of a **shore-power connection** in a traditional boat. Mention the role of an **isolation transformer** and a **galvanic isolator** in the circuit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Full-Load Current:**

$$I_{FL} = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{60}{12} = 5.0\;\text{A}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step (ii) — Cable Cross-Section:**

Allowable voltage drop:

$$V_{drop,allow} = 3\% \times 12\;\text{V} = 0.36\;\text{V}$$

Apply the cable sizing formula:

$$A = \dfrac{I \times 2L \times \rho}{V_{drop}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5.0 \times 2 \times 6 \times 0.0175}{0.36}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$5.0 \times 2 = 10.0$$
$$10.0 \times 6 = 60.0$$
$$60.0 \times 0.0175 = 1.05$$

Final division:

$$A = \dfrac{1.05}{0.36} = 2.917\;\text{mm}^2$$

Round up to the **next standard size** $\Rightarrow$ $A_{selected} = 4.0\;\text{mm}^2$

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for correct rounding to standard size.)*

**Step (iii) — Fuse Size (ABYC 125% Rule):**

$$I_{fuse} = 1.25 \times I_{FL} = 1.25 \times 5.0 = 6.25\;\text{A}$$

Round up to next standard fuse rating $\Rightarrow$ $I_{fuse,selected} = 8.0\;\text{A}$

*(2 marks — 1 for applying 125% rule, 1 for correct standard rating.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected components: 4.0 mm² tinned copper cable, 8A AGC/MRBF fuse.**

**Total for Q(a): 7 marks**

---

**(b) Single-Line Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

The student should draw a single-line diagram showing:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Marina Shore Power Outlet 230V AC] --> B[Shore Power Inlet Socket on Hull]
    B --> C[Main AC Circuit Breaker 16A]
    C --> D[Isolation Transformer 230V to 230V]
    D --> E[Galvanic Isolator in Ground Wire]
    E --> F[Galvanic Isolator]
    F --> G[AC Distribution Panel]
    G --> H1[Outlets and Battery Charger]
    G --> H2[Hot Water Heater Optional]
    D --> I[AC Ground Bus]
    I --> J[Hull Grounding Plate or Underwater Bonding]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Shore Power Inlet** — A marinized male/female socket on the hull side, rated IP67, receives 230V AC from the marina pedestal.
2. **Main AC Circuit Breaker (16A)** — Protects the boat's internal AC wiring from overcurrent; the **first safety device** after shore power enters the boat.
3. **Isolation Transformer (1:1)** — Provides **galvanic isolation** between the marina's ground and the boat's internal AC ground. Prevents stray marina DC currents from entering the boat and corroding underwater metals.
4. **Galvanic Isolator** — A **secondary defense** in the grounding wire, using two diodes in opposition to block low-voltage DC (under 1.4V total) while passing AC fault currents for human safety.
5. **AC Distribution Panel** — Distributes power to onboard AC outlets, water heater, and battery charger.
6. **Hull Bonding / Grounding Plate** — Connects all underwater metal fittings (propeller, shaft, through-hulls) to a common bonding point, equalizing potential and reducing corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both an isolation transformer AND a galvanic isolator?**
> - The **transformer** blocks galvanic DC currents completely.
> - The **isolator** is a cheaper secondary protection that works even when no transformer is fitted.
> - Together, they form a **defense-in-depth** corrosion protection system — a KTU board favorite for 7-mark explanation questions.

**Total for Q(b): 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the KTU ESE:**

- ⚡ **Standard Voltage:** Traditional small boats use **12V DC**; larger vessels use **24V DC**.
- 🔋 **Battery DoD:** Lead-acid = **50%**, Lithium (LiFePO₄) = **80–90%** — do NOT confuse.
- 📏 **Voltage Drop Standard:** **3%** for non-critical DC circuits, **10%** for engine starters (per ABYC E-11).
- 🧮 **Core Formula Trio:** $V = IR$, $P = VI$, $E = Pt$ — these appear in **80%** of numericals.
- 🪢 **Cable Formula:** Always multiply length by **2** for round-trip. Use **$\rho_{Cu} = 0.0175$** for tinned marine copper.
- 🔌 **Fuse Rule:** Rate the fuse at **125%** of full-load current. Round up to next standard size.
- 🚢 **Bus Bars:** Always **two** — one positive (live), one negative (ground/return). Never omit the negative.
- 🛡️ **Corrosion Trio:** Isolation Transformer + Galvanic Isolator + Bonding System = corrosion defense.
- ☀️ **MPPT vs. PWM:** MPPT gives **20–30% more energy** but costs more. Justify choice with numbers in exams.
- 💧 **Bilge Pump:** Must be on a **dedicated circuit** with its own fuse, wired directly to the battery (bypassing the main switch) so it works even when the master switch is OFF.
- 🧭 **Navigation Lights:** Color code — **Port = Red**, **Starboard = Green**, **Stern & Masthead = White**. This is COLREGS-mandated.
- 🌊 **Wire Material:** Always use **tinned copper** in marine environments; bare copper corrodes rapidly in salt air.
- ⚠️ **Common Pitfall:** Never size cables for **current-carrying capacity alone** — always check **voltage drop**; the latter usually governs.
- 🔄 **Energy Flow:** Sources → Charge Controller → Battery → Main Fuse → Bus Bar → Branch Fuses → Loads → Return to Battery Negative.
- 📐 **Units Check:** $1\;\text{Ah} = 1\;\text{A} \times 3600\;\text{s} = 3600\;\text{C}$ (Coulombs). $1\;\text{Wh} = V \times Ah$.
- 🇮🇳 **Kerala Context:** The *Suchitwa Mission* promotes solar-electric traditional boats — a likely essay/short-note topic for Kerala-specific regional questions.
- 🧯 **Safety Priority:** On a boat, **fire at sea = no escape**. The fuse/breaker system is more critical than on land.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Electrical Systems**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PENST864 — Traditional Boat Building Techniques. Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, OBE/NEP 2020 standards, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).*

topics.title: Water and Sanitation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water and Sanitation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Core Concept):**
> In the context of traditional boat building, **Water and Sanitation Systems** refer to the integrated arrangement of structures, compartments, channels, and manual/mechanical devices engineered within a vessel to (a) store, distribute, and preserve fresh water for the crew, (b) safely manage and discharge human waste and greywater, and (c) remove unwanted water (bilge) that accumulates inside the hull, thereby maintaining buoyancy, hygiene, structural integrity, and seaworthiness.

For KTU examination purposes, this topic sits at the intersection of **naval architecture**, **marine hygiene engineering**, and **traditional craft technology**. The 2024 Scheme expects students to be able to distinguish between *fresh water systems*, *bilge systems*, and *sanitation (sewage) systems* onboard traditional vessels such as *vallam*, *uru*, *kettuvallam*, and *dhows*.

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning in Boat-Building Context |
|---|---|
| **Bilge** | Lowest internal region of the hull where water collects |
| **Greywater** | Wastewater from cooking, washing, but not toilet waste |
| **Blackwater** | Human waste (sewage) from toilets |
| **Scuppers** | Openings in the hull side or deck to drain water overboard |
| **Cockpit** | Recessed area where helmsman stands, also serves drainage point |
| **Limber holes** | Small holes at frames that allow bilge water to flow toward the pump |
| **Sea cock** | A valve connecting an internal pipe to the open sea |
| **Cistern** | Water storage tank onboard |
| **Bailer / Bailing** | Manual scooping device to remove water from the bilge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 2 syllabus frames Water and Sanitation Systems as a *holistic onboard resource management* problem — students must know the **traditional methods** (bamboo spouts, coconut-shell bailers, wooden cisterns) alongside the **functional engineering principles** (gravity flow, displacement pumping, siphoning).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a traditional boat as a **floating house**. Just as your home has:
- An overhead tank to store fresh water (the boat has **wooden cisterns** or **bamboo compartments**)
- A drainage pipe taking shower water to the septic tank (the boat has **scuppers** draining greywater overboard)
- A sump in the basement to pump out seepage (the boat has a **bilge** with a pump or bailer)
- A toilet drainage to the septic tank (the boat has a **sanitary tank** or direct-overboard discharge via a sea cock)

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a bathtub floating on a pond. Now imagine a small hole at the bottom — water seeps in continuously. You need three things: (1) a bucket to throw the water out, (2) clean drinking water stored in a sealed jar, and (3) a small chute to throw dirty water directly back into the pond without contaminating your clean water. **The bucket is the bilge pump, the sealed jar is the freshwater cistern, and the chute is the scupper/sanitary discharge.** That is a traditional boat's water and sanitation system in its simplest form.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in Marine Water Systems:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Standard crew water consumption:** $\mathbf{3 \text{ litres/person/day}}$ (minimum survival), $\mathbf{20{-}30 \text{ litres/person/day}}$ (comfortable)
> - **Bilge pump capacity (traditional):** $\mathbf{5{-}15 \text{ litres/minute}}$ (hand-operated)
> - **Freeboard (traditional vallam):** $\mathbf{0.4{-}0.8 \text{ m}}$ — height of hull above waterline, critical for scupper design
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ — used in siphon calculations

These constants appear regularly in KTU numerical problems. Memorize the bolded values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Three Sub-Systems

A traditional vessel's water and sanitation network is best understood as **three interconnected sub-systems**. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks students to "explain the sub-systems" — so the breakdown below is high-yield.

### Sub-System A: Freshwater Storage and Distribution

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Collection:** Rainwater is captured on a stretched canvas/canopy or directly from streams at shore. The water is filtered through a cloth or sand-charcoal layer.
- **Step 2 — Storage:** Water is transferred into **wooden cisterns** (in Kettuvallam) or **bamboo internodes** (in smaller country boats). These are sealed with **coconut-pitch** or **wood-rosin** to prevent leakage.
- **Step 3 — Distribution:** Gravity-fed through a **bamboo spout** or **wooden tap (cork-plugged hole)**. On larger vessels, a small hand-pump (diaphragm type) pressurizes the line.
- **Step 4 — Usage & Replenishment:** Used for drinking, cooking, and washing. Replenished at every shore stop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Principle:** This is a **gravity-fed open-vessel hydraulic system** governed by **Torricelli's Law** at the discharge orifice.

### Sub-System B: Bilge Water Management

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Ingression:** Water enters the hull via plank seams (caulking is imperfect), rain, spray, and minor leaks at mast-step or rudder-gland.
- **Step 2 — Collection at Lowest Point:** Gravity pulls water to the **bilge** (lowest internal hull region).
- **Step 3 — Conveyance via Limber System:** Small **limber holes** drilled at the bottom of frames allow water to flow along the keelson toward the **suction point** of the pump.
- **Step 4 — Removal:** Removed either by (a) **manual bailer** (coconut shell half, tin scoop), (b) **hand-operated diaphragm pump**, or (c) **traditional wooden hand-pump (syringe-type piston pump)**.
- **Step 5 — Discharge Overboard:** Water exits through a **discharge port** above the waterline, often a simple wooden spout.

### Sub-System C: Sanitation (Blackwater & Greywater)

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Containment:** On traditional vessels, a **bucket-style head** (toilet) is the norm. On Kettuvallam (houseboats), a dedicated **sanitary pan** with a wooden lid is used.
- **Step 2 — Storage:** Waste is collected in a **sanitary tank** (varnished wood) or directly in a **removable bucket** with a tight lid.
- **Step 3 — Treatment (Traditional):** Historically, waste was discharged overboard in deep water (now regulated). Some traditional systems use **lime or charcoal** to neutralize before disposal.
- **Step 4 — Greywater (Wash Water) Management:** Routed via **scuppers** (deck-level drain holes) directly overboard; scuppers are positioned just above the waterline to prevent backflow.
- **Step 5 — Periodic Cleaning:** The sanitary tank is **pumped out at shore** — never dumped in shallow backwaters (regulatory and hygienic concern).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate through a scupper/pipe | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Torricelli's velocity at discharge orifice | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Actual discharge through orifice (with $C_d$ coefficient) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ (bilge suction head) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 5 | $V_{req} = n \cdot r \cdot t$ | Freshwater storage volume (crew × rate × time) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 6 | $t_{pump} = V_{bilge} / Q_{pump}$ | Bilge pump-out time | $\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $\Delta F = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{bilge}$ | Loss of buoyancy due to bilge water | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $h_{siphon} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g}$ | Maximum siphon lift (theoretical $\approx 10.3$ m) | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{man}}{P_{input}}$ | Hand-pump hydraulic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{scupper} = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}}$ | Number of scuppers required | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = flow rate, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $v$ = velocity
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (gravitational acceleration)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, typically $\mathbf{0.6{-}0.65}$ for wooden scuppers
- $n$ = number of crew, $r$ = consumption rate, $t$ = voyage duration
- $H_{man}$ = manometric head of pump
- $h$ = head / depth of water

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** These same equations are used by modern naval architects to size bilge pumps on fishing trawlers, to design scupper placements on yachts, and to calculate freshwater tank volume on cargo ships. The traditional boat builder knew them intuitively through experience — the KTU engineer knows them mathematically.

## 2.3 Underlying Engineering Principles

1. **Continuity of Volume:** Water entering the hull must equal water being pumped out, or the vessel will swamp.
2. **Buoyancy Preservation:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$. Every litre of bilge water reduces displaced volume, hence reduces buoyancy.
3. **Hygiene & Disease Control:** Stagnant bilge water breeds mosquitoes and bacteria; scupper flow must prevent pooling.
4. **Freeboard Margin:** The scupper outlet must be positioned $\geq 100 \text{ mm}$ above the design waterline to prevent backflow during roll/pitch.
5. **Siphon Physics:** Atmospheric pressure can lift water a maximum of $\sim 10.3 \text{ m}$ — a siphon-based bilge system is therefore feasible for traditional vessels whose bilge depth is usually $\leq 1 \text{ m}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bilge Pump-Out Time for a Traditional Vessel

**Problem Setup (KTU 2024 typical question):**
A Kettuvallam (houseboat) has bilge water of volume $V_{bilge} = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$ accumulated after a storm. The crew uses a hand-operated diaphragm pump rated at $Q_{pump} = 12 \text{ litres/min}$. Calculate the pump-out time.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

We begin with the fundamental definition of volumetric flow rate.

$$
Q = \frac{V}{t}
$$

This equation simply states that the volume $V$ of fluid passing a point per unit time $t$ is the flow rate $Q$. Rearranging for time:

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q}
$$

**Unit conversion (mandatory in KTU answers — marks are awarded for this):**

$$
V_{bilge} = 0.5 \text{ m}^3 = 0.5 \times 1000 \text{ L} = 500 \text{ L}
$$

The pump rating in litres per minute:

$$
Q_{pump} = 12 \text{ L/min}
$$

Substituting the values into the rearranged equation:

$$
t = \frac{500 \text{ L}}{12 \text{ L/min}}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
t = 41.667 \text{ min}
$$

Rounding to appropriate engineering precision (1 decimal place is standard in KTU scripts):

$$
\boxed{t \approx 41.7 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Sanity check:** This is reasonable — a single crew member with a hand pump can clear the bilge in under an hour.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Freshwater Storage Sizing for a Coastal Voyage

**Problem Setup:**
A traditional fishing boat has a crew of $n = 8$ persons. The voyage lasts $t = 5$ days. Using a comfortable consumption rate of $r = 25 \text{ litres/person/day}$ and including a 20% safety margin, determine the required cistern volume.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The basic freshwater volume required is:

$$
V_{basic} = n \cdot r \cdot t
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
V_{basic} = 8 \times 25 \times 5
$$

Multiplying stepwise:

$$
V_{basic} = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ L}
$$

Applying the 20% safety margin (multiply by 1.20):

$$
V_{req} = V_{basic} \times 1.20
$$

$$
V_{req} = 1000 \times 1.20 = 1200 \text{ L}
$$

Converting to cubic metres (since 1000 L = 1 m³):

$$
\boxed{V_{req} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3}
$$

**Design implication:** A wooden cistern of internal dimensions $1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$ will suffice. Traditional builders would build two smaller cisterns (for stability and redundancy) of $0.6 \text{ m}^3$ each.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Scupper Sizing Using Torricelli's Law

**Problem Setup:**
A traditional vessel has a deck area of $A_{deck} = 20 \text{ m}^2$. During heavy rain, water accumulates at a rate of $Q_{in} = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. The scupper outlet is $h = 0.5 \text{ m}$ above the waterline. Each scupper is a circular hole of diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm}$. Using $C_d = 0.62$, find the number of scuppers required.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The discharge through a single scupper (using the orifice formula from Section 2) is:

$$
q = C_d \cdot A_{scupper} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

Compute the area of one scupper (circular):

$$
A_{scupper} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{scupper} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = \frac{0.007854}{4} = 0.001963 \text{ m}^2
$$

Now compute the velocity head (Torricelli):

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.5}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132 \text{ m/s}
$$

Substituting into the discharge formula:

$$
q = 0.62 \times 0.001963 \times 3.132
$$

Multiplying stepwise:

$$
0.62 \times 0.001963 = 0.001217
$$

$$
q = 0.001217 \times 3.132 = 0.003812 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

The number of scuppers required is the inflow rate divided by the single-scupper discharge:

$$
N = \frac{Q_{in}}{q} = \frac{0.005}{0.003812}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 1.31}
$$

Since you cannot have 0.31 of a scupper, **round up to 2 scuppers** — this is standard engineering practice and the KTU examiner expects it.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Siphon Feasibility for Bilge Emptying

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The theoretical maximum lift of a siphon column is given by:

$$
h_{max} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g}
$$

Substituting $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (freshwater), and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$
h_{max} = \frac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_{max} = \frac{101325}{9810}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{max} \approx 10.33 \text{ m}}
$$

For a traditional boat with a bilge depth of $\sim 1 \text{ m}$, a siphon-based bilge emptying system is **trivially feasible** with a large safety margin — this is why traditional designers could empty bilge water using a simple hose with no pump at all.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Traditional Bilge Removal Methods

| Method | Flow Rate (L/min) | Crew Effort | Capital Cost | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coconut-shell bailer | $5{-}8$ | High (continuous) | Negligible | High — history of Keralan boat-building |
| Tin scoop | $8{-}10$ | High | Negligible | Medium — modern adaptation |
| Hand diaphragm pump | $12{-}20$ | Moderate | Low | **High — frequently asked** |
| Wooden piston pump (traditional) | $15{-}25$ | Moderate | Medium | High — traditional craft technology |
| Siphon hose | $20{-}40$ | Low (once primed) | Negligible | Medium — engineering principle |
| Electric bilge pump (modern) | $50{-}150$ | None | High | Low — outside traditional scope |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
