
### 4.4 Functional Notes on the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> - **Diagram 1** maps the **load path**: environmental forces → vessel → lines → termination points (quay, buoy, pile, anchor) → equilibrium.
> - **Diagram 2** shows the **complete taxonomy** of berth and mooring types with sub-classification of SPM into CALM and SALM.
> - **Diagram 3** contextualizes the **Kerala traditional mooring** — coir ropes, wooden piles, stone anchors, and natural bank bollards — within the same engineering framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Define the term "berth" as used in traditional and modern boat building. Differentiate between "berth" and "mooring."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **berth** is a designated location in a harbour, port, marina, or waterway where a vessel is secured for cargo handling, passenger transfer, or shelter. A **mooring** is the complete physical system — lines, anchors, buoys, bollards — used to hold the vessel in position. The berth is the *location*; the mooring is the *method/equipment* used to secure the vessel at that location. Together they form the "berthing and mooring system."

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **List any four types of berths and state one characteristic feature of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Alongside berth:** Vessel parallel to the quay; secured with head, stern, and spring lines.
2. **Mediterranean berth (Med-Mo):** Stern tied to the quay; bow anchored offshore; boat lies at ~45°.
3. **Finger pontoon berth:** Floating walkway with perpendicular fingers; common in modern marinas.
4. **Dry berth:** Vessel stored on land cradle; used for repairs and anti-fouling.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Classify the different types of berths used in ports and marinas, with suitable sketches. Explain the Mediterranean mooring technique in detail. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of (i) Single Point Mooring (SPM) and (ii) Multi-leg Spread Mooring. Compare their applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Q.A(a):**

**Classification of Berths (4 marks):**

1. **Alongside Berth:** Vessel lies parallel to the quay. Lines used: head line, stern line, fore-spring, aft-spring. Used for cargo vessels, ferries, naval ships.

2. **Perpendicular Berth:** Vessel lies perpendicular (bow toward quay). Used for small craft, fishing boats, Ro-Ro operations.

3. **Mediterranean Berth (Med-Mo):** Bow anchored offshore, stern tied to quay. Boat lies at ~45° to the dock. Saves marina space; no finger pontoon required.

4. **Finger Pontoon Berth:** Floating walkways with perpendicular fingers projecting from a main pontoon. Each vessel slots between two fingers. Used in modern marinas.

5. **Stern-to / Bow-to Berth:** Stern (or bow) is held against the dock; both anchors dropped on the seaward side. Used in narrow channels.

6. **Dry Berth:** Vessel is hauled out onto a cradle on land. Used for storage, hull cleaning, and repairs.

**Mediterranean Mooring — Detailed (3 marks):**

In Med-mo, the boat drops its **bow anchor** first, then reverses toward the quay while paying out the anchor chain. Once the stern is close to the dock, two **stern lines** (port and starboard) are passed to shore bollards, pulling the stern snug against the quay. The boat now lies at ~45° to the dock, with the bow held offshore by the anchor and the stern held inshore by the lines. To depart, the stern lines are released, and the boat drives forward off the anchor. This system is space-efficient and does not require finger pontoons.

**Diagram (descriptive — to be drawn on exam sheet):**
```
   [Quay]----------------------[Bollard]
                              /  \
                       Stern-1   Stern-2
                              \  /
                               \/
                              [Boat]
                              / 
                          Bow Anchor
                             /
                            /
                       [Seabed]
```

**Model Answer for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Single Point Mooring — SPM (3.5 marks):**
A Single Point Mooring secures a vessel at **one point** in open water, allowing it to **weathervane** (rotate) freely with wind and current. Two main types:
- **CALM (Catenary Anchor Leg Mooring):** A large buoy held to the seabed by 4–6 catenary anchor chains. The vessel ties its mooring hawsers to the buoy's top.
- **SALM (Single Anchor Leg Mooring):** A rigid tower fixed to the seabed by a single articulated base; the vessel ties to the top of the tower.

Used primarily for **oil tankers** at offshore terminals. Allows 300,000+ DWT tankers to load/discharge via floating hoses.

**(ii) Multi-leg Spread Mooring (3.5 marks):**
The vessel is restrained by **6 to 12 mooring lines** radiating outward to anchors/piles on the seabed. Lines are grouped into **3 or 4 clusters** at 120° or 90° spacing. The vessel has limited weathervaning ability; heading is approximately fixed.
Used for **FPSOs (Floating Production Storage & Offloading)**, semi-submersible drilling rigs, and SPAR platforms. Lines may be **chain, wire, or polyester rope**, arranged as **catenary** or **taut** systems.

**Comparison:**
| Aspect | SPM | Spread Mooring |
|---|---|---|
| Anchor points | 1 (or group) | 6–12 |
| Weathervaning | Free | Restricted |
| Heading control | None (free) | Approximately fixed |
| Application | Oil tankers | FPSO, rigs |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the various types of mooring systems used for traditional and modern boats. Give examples from Kerala's traditional boat-building context. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A mooring chain has a weight per unit length $w = 80$ N/m. The horizontal tension at the fairlead is $T_H = 40$ kN, and the fairlead is at a height of 24 m above the seabed touch-down point. Using the catenary equation, determine the horizontal length of the suspended chain. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Q.B(a):**

**Types of Mooring Systems (5 marks):**

1. **Buoy (Swing) Mooring:** A heavy buoy permanently anchored to a chain + sinker on the seabed. Vessels tie to the buoy and swing freely. Common in Cochin backwaters.

2. **Trot Mooring:** A row of buoys linked by ground chains, with a long shore-line connecting the shore-end buoy to a land bollard. Each vessel takes one buoy.

3. **Pile (Dolphin) Mooring:** Wooden or concrete piles driven into the seabed. Vessels tie directly to the piles or to wooden caps (dolphins). Traditional Kerala backwaters use **bamboo and wooden piles**.

4. **Anchor Mooring:** The vessel drops its own anchors (typically 2 in a riding pattern) to the seabed. Used in temporary anchorage.

5. **Single Point Mooring (SPM):** Offshore terminal mooring with one secure point; used for tankers.

6. **Multi-leg Spread Mooring:** Multiple anchor legs for offshore platforms and FPSOs.

7. **Catenary Mooring:** Lines hang in a catenary curve, providing restoring force from suspended weight.

8. **Taut Mooring:** Lines are pre-tensioned straight; restoring force comes from line elasticity.

**Kerala Traditional Examples (2 marks):**

- **Coir rope mooring** (made from coconut husk fibre) on *kettuvallam* and *vallam* boats — strong, elastic, salt-water resistant.
- **Wooden pile moorings** driven into Vembanad Lake mud for houseboat stations.
- **Stone anchors (kuzhi-mani)** — flat stone weights with rope holes, used in older fishing craft.
- **Coconut tree bollards** on the backwater banks serving as natural mooring posts.

**Model Answer for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $w = 80$ N/m
- $T_H = 40$ kN = 40,000 N
- $h_f = 24$ m

**Step 1 — Compute the catenary parameter:**

$$
a = \frac{T_H}{w} = \frac{40{,}000}{80} = 500 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the catenary height equation:**

$$
h_f = a \left[ \cosh\left(\frac{L_H}{a}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

$$
24 = 500 \left[ \cosh\left(\frac{L_H}{500}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the hyperbolic term:**

$$
\cosh\left(\frac{L_H}{500}\right) = 1 + \frac{24}{500} = 1.048
$$

**Step 4 — Apply inverse hyperbolic cosine using the identity:**

$$
\cosh^{-1}(x) = \ln\left( x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1} \right)
$$

$$
\cosh^{-1}(1.048) = \ln\left( 1.048 + \sqrt{1.048^2 - 1} \right) = \ln(1.048 + \sqrt{0.0983})
$$

$$
= \ln(1.048 + 0.3135) = \ln(1.3615) \approx 0.3089
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for horizontal length:**

$$
L_H = 500 \times 0.3089 = 154.45 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$
y = 500 \left[ \cosh(0.3089) - 1 \right] = 500 \times (1.0481 - 1) = 500 \times 0.0481 = 24.05 \text{ m} \approx 24 \text{ m} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Result:** $L_H \approx \mathbf{154.5 \text{ m}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often forget to convert kN to N before computing $a$. This causes $a$ to be off by a factor of 1000. Always check units first.
> 2. **Identity confusion:** Some students mistakenly use $\ln(1 + x)$ instead of $\ln(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})$ for $\cosh^{-1}(x)$. This gives a wrong answer.
> 3. **Classification overlap:** Students mix up "berth" types and "mooring" types in classification questions. Remember — berths are **location-based**; moorings are **system-based**.
> 4. **Missing traditional context:** For questions on Kerala boats, marks are awarded for **traditional examples** (coir rope, stone anchors, wooden piles). Generic answers without Kerala context lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sketch omission:** KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions if no diagram/schematic is provided. Always include labelled diagrams.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Types of Berth/Moorings**

- ✅ **Berth = location** (the slot); **Mooring = the system** (the equipment). Don't confuse them.
- ✅ **Alongside berth** uses **head, stern, and spring lines** — minimum 4 lines.
- ✅ **Mediterranean berth (Med-Mo)** uses **bow anchor + 2 stern lines**; boat lies at ~45°.
- ✅ **Finger pontoon berth** is standard for **modern recreational marinas**.
- ✅ **Swing mooring** lets the boat **weathervane 360°**; ideal for yacht clubs.
- ✅ **Trot mooring** uses a **row of buoys** with a **shore-line** to land.
- ✅ **Pile mooring** uses **driven piles** (wood, steel, or concrete).
- ✅ **Single Point Mooring (SPM)** — **CALM** = catenary buoy; **SALM** = rigid tower.
- ✅ **Spread mooring** uses **6–12 lines**; for FPSOs and offshore rigs.
- ✅ **Catenary mooring** — restoring force from **gravity**; deep water.
- ✅ **Taut mooring** — restoring force from **elasticity**; smaller footprint.
- ✅ **Kerala traditional:** coir ropes, wooden piles, stone anchors, coconut tree bollards.
- ✅ **Standard formula:** $a = T_H / w$ is the **catenary parameter** — memorize it.
- ✅ **Catenary equation:** $y = a[\cosh(x/a) - 1]$ — used in **derivation problems**.
- ✅ **Mooring line pretension** ≈ **3% of MBL**; **SWL** ≈ **50% of MBL**.
- ✅ **Under-Keel Clearance (UKC)** ≥ **10% of vessel draft** for safe berthing.
- ✅ Always **draw a labelled diagram** for full marks in KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sea Trials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sea Trials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sea Trials (also called "Builder's Trials" or "Acceptance Trials"):** A comprehensive series of controlled on-water performance evaluations conducted on a newly constructed or refitted traditional vessel to verify that the boat meets its **design specifications**, **regulatory safety standards**, and **operational fitness** before being handed over to the owner or operator.

In the context of **PENST864 – Traditional Boat Building Techniques**, sea trials are not merely modern performance benchmarks. They are a **continuation of an ancient craft-validation ritual** that traditional boat builders (e.g., the *Vishwakarma* community of Kerala who built *kettuvallams*, *vallams*, *odams*) have practiced for centuries — launching a boat into its element to "listen" to how the hull behaves in real water, real wind, and real wave conditions.

**Per KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcomes, sea trials assess:**
1. **Hull integrity** (watertightness, leak detection).
2. **Propulsion efficiency** (speed under sail/oar/paddle/auxiliary engine).
3. **Maneuverability** (turning circle, course stability).
4. **Stability** (heeling, righting moment, freeboard behaviour).
5. **Habitability & safety** (deck space, bilge pumping, lifesaving equipment).
6. **Endurance / fuel/energy range** for powered or hybrid vessels.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of sea trials as a "Final Medical Check-up" for a newborn boat.**

Just as a doctor runs a battery of tests on a newborn baby (heart rate, breathing, reflexes, weight, hearing) before declaring the child healthy, a naval architect and master shipwright run a battery of *physical performance tests* on a freshly-built boat before declaring it "born fit for the sea."

**A simpler daily-life analogy:**
Imagine you have just stitched together a handmade kite. Before flying it on a windy day, you will:
1. Pull the string firmly to test tensile strength (hull structural test).
2. Hold it up in mild breeze to see if it balances (stability test).
3. Run short distances with it to gauge lift and stability (preliminary trial).
4. Only then fly it in real wind conditions (full sea trial).

Sea trials are exactly this — but for a vessel that may carry lives, cargo, and livelihoods.

---

## 1.3 Standard Units & Metrics (KTU-Mandated)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed | $V$ | **Knot (kn)** | 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s** = **1.852 km/h** |
| Distance | $s$ | **Nautical Mile (NM)** | 1 NM = **1852 m** |
| Displacement | $\Delta$ | **Tonne (t)** | Mass of water displaced |
| Heel angle | $\phi$ | **Degrees (°)** | Roll of vessel |
| Trim angle | $\theta$ | **Degrees (°)** | Longitudinal tilt |
| Metacentric height | $GM$ | **Metre (m)** | Stability indicator |
| Righting moment | $GZ$ | **Tonne-metre (t·m)** | Restoring torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed in traditional boat trials is conventionally measured in KNOTS, not km/h.** This is because 1 nautical mile = 1 minute of latitude, which simplifies dead-reckoning navigation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown of Sea Trials

A sea trial for a traditional vessel proceeds through a **structured sequence** (often codified by classification societies like IRS — Indian Register of Shipping; or by state maritime boards for KTU-validated modules):

### Step 1: Pre-Departure Inspection (Static / Dockside)
- Visual hull inspection for planking gaps (especially in *carvel-built* or *kerala stitched-plank* boats).
- Caulking check — cotton/ coconut-fibre seams examined for tightness.
- Sail, mast, oar and rigging integrity check (for sail/oar vessels like *vallam* or *uru*).
- Bilge sounding — verify dry bilge or measure any seepage rate in litres/hour.

### Step 2: In-Shelter Trial (Manoeuvring Basin)
- Conducted in a calm bay or backwater area (e.g., **Vembanad Lake, Cochin Harbour basin**).
- Tests low-speed handling — turns, reverse (for powered vessels), propeller/rudder response.

### Step 3: Open-Water Trial (Measured Mile)
- Run along a known straight course (measured mile / measured kilometer) at increasing RPMs.
- For oar/sail vessels: timed runs over a fixed course at controlled paddle cadence.
- Records **speed vs. effort** (oar strokes/min or sail trim setting).

### Step 4: Manoeuvring Trial
- Turning circle (advance, transfer, tactical diameter).
- Crash stop / stopping distance.
- Zig-zag (Kempf / Dieudonné) for powered vessels — not always applicable to pure traditional craft.

### Step 5: Stability & Heeling Trial
- Static heel test (weights placed on one side, heel angle measured with inclinometer).
- Free-running heel in turns.
- Recovery from heel.

### Step 6: Endurance & Range Trial
- For motorised traditional boats (*motorised kettuvallam*, *tourist houseboats*): continuous run at cruising RPM until fuel reserve reached.
- For sail/oar: paddler fatigue cycle, sail endurance in moderate wind.

### Step 7: Return & Post-Trial Inspection
- Hull re-check for leaks developed during the run.
- Engine temperature, shaft bearing temperatures logged.
- Trial data plotted into the **Builder's Trial Report**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars in tables. Never use `|`.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed (basic) | $V = \dfrac{s}{t}$ | $V$ in knots, $s$ in NM, $t$ in hours | All timed-run trials |
| 2 | Knot conversion | $V_{\text{km/h}} = 1.852 \cdot V_{\text{kn}}$ | Conversion | Cross-checking log values |
| 3 | m/s conversion | $V_{\text{m/s}} = 0.5144 \cdot V_{\text{kn}}$ | Conversion | SI unit requirement |
| 4 | Displacement (Archimedes) | $\Delta = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{w} \cdot g}$ | $\Delta$ in $m^3$, $W$ in N, $\rho_{w}$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81$ | Hull buoyancy check |
| 5 | Reserve buoyancy | $RB = \dfrac{V_{\text{fl}}}{V_{\text{d}}} \times 100\%$ | % freeboard reserve | Safety margin |
| 6 | Turning circle (advance) | $A_d = R \cdot \sin\psi$ | $\psi$ = rudder angle, $R$ = turning radius | Manoeuvring trial |
| 7 | Tactical diameter | $T_d \approx 2R$ | m | Manoeuvring trial |
| 8 | Stopping distance (rough) | $s_{\text{stop}} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{2a}$ | $a$ = decel in $\text{m/s}^2$ | Crash-stop trial |
| 9 | Righting lever | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$ | $\phi$ = heel angle | Stability trial |
| 10 | Metacentric height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | all in m (above keel) | Stability trial |
| 11 | Heel angle from weight shift | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot GM}$ | $w \cdot d$ = shift moment | Static heel test |
| 12 | Power required | $P = R_{T} \cdot V$ | $R_T$ = total resistance (N), $V$ in m/s | Self-propulsion trial |
| 13 | Fuel endurance | $T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{cap}}}{\text{FCR} \cdot P}$ | hours | Range trial |
| 14 | Seepage rate (traditional hull) | $Q_{s} = \dfrac{V_{\text{leak}}}{t_{\text{obs}}}$ | litres/hour | Leak test |
| 15 | Block coefficient (typical traditional hull) | $C_{B} = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Hull-form check |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **modern heritage-boat tourism** (e.g., Kerala houseboats operating on Vembanad Lake), sea-trial data is the *legal baseline* for:
- Issuing **State Inland Waterways Department** fitness certificates.
- Insurance underwriting.
- Crew certification (each traditional boat must have a trial report on board).
- KTU student projects where traditional boats are designed, built, and tested as part of the B.Tech capstone.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions**, examiners expect you to write the formula **AND** substitute values **AND** state the units. Skipping units = −1 mark penalty routinely applied by KTU valuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed in Knots from a Measured-Mile Run

**Scenario:** A newly-built *kettuvallam* (houseboat) is run over a **measured nautical mile** in **Cochin backwaters**. The bow passes the first marker at $t_1 = 09:14:30$ and the second marker at $t_2 = 09:18:42$.

### Step 1: Compute elapsed time in seconds
$$
t = t_2 - t_1 = 18\,\text{min}\,42\,\text{s} - 14\,\text{min}\,30\,\text{s} = 4\,\text{min}\,12\,\text{s}
$$

### Step 2: Convert to hours
$$
t = \dfrac{4 \times 60 + 12}{3600} = \dfrac{252}{3600} = 0.07\,\text{h}
$$

### Step 3: Apply $V = s/t$
$$
V = \dfrac{1\,\text{NM}}{0.07\,\text{h}} = 14.286\,\text{kn}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to SI (for KTU cross-check)
$$
V_{\text{km/h}} = 1.852 \times 14.286 = 26.46\,\text{km/h}
$$

$$
V_{\text{m/s}} = 0.5144 \times 14.286 = 7.348\,\text{m/s}
$$

> **Result:** Trial speed ≈ **14.29 knots (≈ 26.46 km/h)** — well above a typical Kerala *vallam* cruising speed (4–6 kn), confirming the auxiliary engine is performing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Turning Circle Tactical Diameter

**Scenario:** During the manoeuvring trial, the boat turns with rudder hard-over at $35°$. Observed steady-state turning radius $R = 42$ m.

### Step 1: Tactical diameter definition
$$
T_d \approx 2R
$$

### Step 2: Substitute
$$
T_d = 2 \times 42 = 84\,\text{m}
$$

### Step 3: Advance distance (rudder angle $\psi = 35°$)
$$
A_d = R \cdot \sin\psi = 42 \times \sin(35°) = 42 \times 0.5736 = 24.09\,\text{m}
$$

> **Result:** The boat advances ~24 m forward before completing a 90° turn, and the complete loop is ~84 m wide. Acceptable for inland backwater navigation where channel width is typically 50–100 m.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Righting Moment & Heel Angle

**Scenario:** A 4-tonne traditional fishing boat ($W = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24$ kN) is heeled in the static stability trial. Given: $KG = 1.2$ m, $KB = 0.8$ m, $BM = 0.7$ m.

### Step 1: Metacentric height
$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 0.8 + 0.7 - 1.2 = 0.3\,\text{m}
$$

### Step 2: Heel angle from a shift of crew weight ($w = 800$ N, $d = 1.5$ m to one side)
$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot g \cdot GM} = \dfrac{800 \times 1.5}{4000 \times 9.81 \times 0.3}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{1200}{11772} = 0.1019
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.1019) = 5.82°
$$

### Step 3: Righting lever at this heel
$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi = 0.3 \times \sin(5.82°) = 0.3 \times 0.1014 = 0.0304\,\text{m}
$$

### Step 4: Righting moment
$$
M_{R} = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ = 4000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0304 = 1192.7\,\text{N·m}
$$

> **Result:** A small but positive righting moment — the boat returns upright. If $GZ$ had been negative, the boat would capsize — **failing the trial**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Displacement (Archimedes Verification)

**Scenario:** A new wooden *vallam* of mass $m = 1500$ kg is floated in seawater ($\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$).

### Step 1: Weight
$$
W = m \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715\,\text{N}
$$

### Step 2: Archimedes — volume of water displaced
$$
\nabla = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{14715}{1025 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{14715}{10055.25} = 1.463\,\text{m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Convert to tonnes (1 m³ seawater ≈ 1.025 t)
$$
\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho = 1.463 \times 1025 = 1499.6\,\text{kg} \approx 1.5\,\text{t}
$$

> **Result:** Displacement matches design mass within 0.03% — hull-volume calculation is **validated**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Sea Trial Results for a KTU-Built Traditional Vallam

| Parameter | Design Specification | Trial Observed | Pass / Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trial speed (loaded) | 5.0 kn | 5.4 kn | ✅ Pass |
| Turning circle (35° rudder) | ≤ 90 m | 84 m | ✅ Pass |
| Stopping distance (from 5 kn) | ≤ 25 m | 21 m | ✅ Pass |
| Bilge seepage | ≤ 2 L/h | 0.6 L/h | ✅ Pass |
| Heel angle (crew shift) | ≤ 10° | 5.82° | ✅ Pass |
| Engine temp (after 1 h) | ≤ 85 °C | 78 °C | ✅ Pass |
| Fuel endurance | ≥ 6 h | 6.5 h | ✅ Pass |

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Full Numerical — Speed-Power Curve

A traditional boat-builder student runs trials at three engine RPMs and logs speed. Compute the required propulsive power at each RPM.

**Data:** Total resistance at each speed approximated by $R_T = 1800 \cdot V^2$ (N, V in m/s).

| RPM | Trial speed (kn) | Trial speed (m/s) | $R_T$ (N) | $P = R_T V$ (W) | $P$ (HP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1200 | 4.0 | 2.058 | $1800 \times 4.235 = 7623$ | $7623 \times 2.058 = 15688$ | 21.0 |
| 1800 | 6.5 | 3.344 | $1800 \times 11.18 = 20127$ | $20127 \times 3.344 = 67300$ | 90.2 |
| 2400 | 8.7 | 4.475 | $1800 \times 20.02 = 36043$ | $36043 \times 4.475 = 161292$ | 216.3 |

**Conversion used:** $\text{HP} = \text{W} / 745.7$.

> This power curve is the **single most important deliverable** of a sea trial for an engine-fitted traditional vessel. It validates that the installed engine matches the hull's resistance — neither under-powered nor over-powered.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sea Trial Procedural Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boat Construction Complete] --> B[Pre-Departure Static Inspection]
    B --> B1[Hull Visual Check]
    B --> B2[Caulking and Seams]
    B --> B3[Bilge Sounding]
    B --> B4[Rigging and Sail Check]
    B1 --> C[In-Shelter Trial]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Low Speed Turns]
    C --> C2[Reverse Check]
    C --> C3[Rudder Response]
    C1 --> D[Measured Mile Open Water Trial]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Speed vs RPM Curve]
    D --> D2[Oar Cadence Log for Non Powered]
    D --> D3[Sail Trim Log for Sail Vessels]
    D1 --> E[Manoeuvring Trial]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Turning Circle Advance and Transfer]
    E --> E2[Stopping Distance]
    E --> E3[Zig Zag if Powered]
    E1 --> F[Stability Trial]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Static Heel with Test Weights]
    F --> F2[Free Running Heel in Turns]
    F --> F3[Recovery Check]
    F1 --> G[Endurance and Range Trial]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Fuel Burn Curve if Powered]
    G --> G2[Paddler Fatigue Cycle if Manual]
    G1 --> H[Return to Berth and Post Trial Leak Check]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Compile Builder Trial Report]
    I --> J{All Parameters Within Spec?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Boat Accepted and Delivered]
    J -->|No| L[Rectify Defects and Re Trial]
    L --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Sea Trial Data Acquisition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Onboard_Sensors
        S1[GPS Speed Log]
        S2[Inclinometer for Heel]
        S3[Bilge Level Sensor]
        S4[Engine RPM and Temperature]
        S5[Fuel Flow Meter]
        S6[Wind Anemometer]
    end

    subgraph Data_Logger
        DL[Central Logger and Time Stamped Recorder]
    end

    subgraph Post_Trial_Analysis
        PA1[Speed Power Curve Plot]
        PA2[Turning Circle Diagram]
        PA3[Stability GZ Curve]
        PA4[Seepage Rate Trend]
        PA5[Fuel Endurance Chart]
    end

    S1 --> DL
    S2 --> DL
    S3 --> DL
    S4 --> DL
    S5 --> DL
    S6 --> DL

    DL --> PA1
    DL --> PA2
    DL --> PA3
    DL --> PA4
    DL --> PA5
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Forces Acting on a Traditional Vessel During a Turning Trial

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Forces_On_Vessel
        F1[Weight W acting downward at G]
        F2[Buoyancy B acting upward at B]
        F3[Centripetal Force during turn acting outward at G]
        F4[Hydrodynamic Side Force from hull and keel acting inward at C]
    end

    F1 --> R1[Net Heeling Moment]
    F2 --> R1
    F3 --> R1
    F4 --> R1
    R1 --> O[Heel Angle Phi measured by inclinometer]
    O --> V[Validation Against Design GZ Curve]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define "Sea Trials" in the context of traditional boat building. List any four parameters verified during a sea trial.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Sea trials are a series of controlled on-water performance tests conducted on a newly-built or refitted traditional boat to verify that the vessel meets its design specifications, statutory safety standards, and operational fitness for handover to the owner.

**Four parameters (1 mark — any four):**
1. Hull integrity and watertightness (seepage rate).
2. Speed under specified power/oar/sail.
3. Manoeuvrability (turning circle, stopping distance).
4. Stability (heel angle, righting moment).
5. Fuel/paddler endurance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**State Archimedes' principle as applied to displacement calculation of a traditional wooden boat. Give the formula with units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):**
Archimedes' Principle states that a floating body displaces a volume of water whose weight is exactly equal to the weight of the body. Hence, a boat of weight $W$ floating in water of density $\rho_w$ displaces a volume $\nabla$ such that:

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

where $W$ is in Newtons (N), $\rho_w$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, and $\nabla$ in $\text{m}^3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure** of conducting a sea trial for a newly-built traditional fishing *vallam* of length 8 m fitted with a small outboard engine. Include all six standard phases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During the measured-mile trial, the boat covered **1 nautical mile in 4 minutes 30 seconds**. Calculate its speed in **(i) knots, (ii) km/h, and (iii) m/s**. Comment on whether this speed is acceptable for a coastal fishing *vallam* (typical design speed 5–6 knots).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure [7 marks]:**

1. **Phase 1 — Pre-Departure Static Inspection (1 mark)**
   Hull visual check for planking gaps; caulking of cotton/coconut-fibre seams; bilge sounding for dryness; outboard engine mount, fuel line, and steering tiller inspection; safety equipment (life jackets, oars, bailer) verification.

2. **Phase 2 — In-Shelter / Harbour Trial (1 mark)**
   Conducted in calm backwater area. Low-speed manoeuvres; reverse gear test; tiller response; engine warming up to operating temperature; cooling-water outlet check.

3. **Phase 3 — Measured-Mile Open-Water Trial (1.5 marks)**
   Run along a fixed measured mile at progressive RPMs (e.g., 1500, 2500, 3500, full throttle). Time recorded at each end; speed-log cross-checked.

4. **Phase 4 — Manoeuvring Trial (1 mark)**
   Hard-over turning circle recorded (advance, transfer, tactical diameter). Crash-stop from full speed to neutral measured. Course-keeping stability noted.

5. **Phase 5 — Stability / Heeling Trial (1.5 marks)**
   Static heel test — known weights shifted across the boat; heel angle measured with inclinometer. Free-running heel during sharp turns recorded. Recovery verified.

6. **Phase 6 — Endurance, Return & Post-Trial Inspection (1 mark)**
   Continuous run at cruising RPM for fuel endurance (typically 1–2 hours). Return to berth; hull re-inspected for leaks developed; engine temperature, oil pressure logged; all data entered into Builder's Trial Report.

**(b) Speed Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- Distance $s = 1\,\text{NM}$
- Time $t = 4\,\text{min}\,30\,\text{s} = 270\,\text{s} = 0.075\,\text{h}$

**(i) Speed in knots:**
$$
V = \dfrac{s}{t} = \dfrac{1}{0.075} = 13.33\,\text{kn}
$$

**(ii) Speed in km/h:**
$$
V_{\text{km/h}} = 1.852 \times 13.33 = 24.69\,\text{km/h}
$$

**(iii) Speed in m/s:**
$$
V_{\text{m/s}} = 0.5144 \times 13.33 = 6.857\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Comment (3 marks allocation in part b):**
The computed speed of **13.33 knots (24.69 km/h)** is **significantly higher** than the typical design cruising speed of 5–6 knots for a coastal fishing *vallam*. This suggests either (a) the outboard engine is over-spec for the hull, leading to planing behaviour and poor fuel economy, or (b) the measured run benefited from tidal current assistance and is not the boat's free-running speed. A corrected trial against tide or at mid-tide slack should be re-run. For a coastal fishing application, sustained high speeds cause hull slamming and shorten the boat's service life — the trial result should be **discussed with the boat-owner before acceptance**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **importance of stability trials** for traditional boats. Define **metacentric height ($GM$)** and derive the relationship $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$. State its significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A traditional *uru* (Arabian Sea dhow-type vessel) of displacement **80 tonnes** has $KG = 1.8$ m, $KB = 1.1$ m, $BM = 1.0$ m. Compute **(i) metacentric height, (ii) the heel angle when 4 crew members of total mass 280 kg shift to one side at a transverse distance of 1.2 m, and (iii) the righting moment at this heel angle**. Comment on the result.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory of Stability Trials [7 marks]:**

**Importance (3 marks):**
1. Traditional wooden boats, especially those operating in rough monsoonal sea conditions off the Kerala coast, must not capsize under crew-movement or wave-induced heeling. Stability trials validate this.
2. Asymmetric loading (catch on one side, crew movement, gust of wind) creates heeling moments; the trial quantifies the restoring (righting) moment.
3. Insurance, classification, and KTU project-evaluation all require documented $GM$ and $GZ$ data.
4. Stability affects every other trial — speed, turning, endurance are meaningless if the boat cannot stay upright.

**Definition of GM (2 marks):**
The metacentric height $GM$ is the vertical distance between the centre of gravity ($G$) and the metacentre ($M$) of a floating vessel. It is the **primary geometric indicator of initial transverse stability** — positive $GM$ means stable; large $GM$ means stiff (jerky), small $GM$ means tender (sloppy).

**Derivation of $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$ (2 marks):**
For small heeling angles, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$ along an arc of radius $BM$ centred at $M$. The line of action of buoyancy now intersects the centreline at $Z$ (the "zero moment point"). The righting lever is the horizontal distance $GZ$.

In triangle $GZM'$ (where $M'$ is the projection of $M$ onto the original centreline):

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi
$$

This is exact for small angles (up to ~10°) and is a good approximation for larger angles when corrected by a sine series.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta = 80\,\text{t} = 80000\,\text{kg}$
- $KG = 1.8\,\text{m}$, $KB = 1.1\,\text{m}$, $BM = 1.0\,\text{m}$
- Crew mass $w_m = 280\,\text{kg}$, shift distance $d = 1.2\,\text{m}$

**(i) Metacentric height:**
$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 1.1 + 1.0 - 1.8 = 0.3\,\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Heel angle:**
$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{w_m \cdot g \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot g \cdot GM} = \dfrac{280 \times 9.81 \times 1.2}{80000 \times 9.81 \times 0.3}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{3296.16}{235440} = 0.014
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.014) = 0.80°
$$

**(iii) Righting moment at this heel:**
$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi = 0.3 \times \sin(0.80°) = 0.3 \times 0.01396 = 4.189 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
M_R = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ = 80000 \times 9.81 \times 4.189 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
M_R = 3287.5\,\text{N·m} = 3.29\,\text{kN·m}
$$

**Comment (3 marks allocation):**
The vessel shows very small heel (0.80°) and a healthy positive righting moment (3.29 kN·m). However, $GM = 0.3$ m is **marginally low** for a 80-tonne ocean-going *uru*. While the boat is stable in calm conditions, it may be **too tender in rough monsoon seas**. A traditional shipwright would either (i) lower the centre of gravity by relocating heavy machinery/ballast lower in the hull, or (ii) accept a slightly stiffer ($GM \approx 0.6$ m) boat for offshore work.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Sea-Trial Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — KTU valuators deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** per quantity without units. Always write `kn`, `m/s`, `t·m`, etc.
> 2. **Confusing knots with km/h** — 1 knot = 1.852 km/h, NOT 1 km/h. This is the **most common single error** in PENST864 valuation reports.
> 3. **Skipping the "comment/discussion" step** in numerical problems — KTU explicitly awards 2–3 marks for engineering interpretation; pure arithmetic gets only 4 out of 7.
> 4. **Using $GM$ without checking its sign** — if $GM$ comes out negative, the boat is unstable and the trial has FAILED. Students often plug numbers in without this critical observation.
> 5. **Mixing up displacement ($\Delta$) and volume of displacement ($\nabla$)** — $\Delta$ is mass (tonnes), $\nabla$ is volume ($\text{m}^3$). They are related but not interchangeable.
> 6. **Not mentioning safety equipment checks** in the procedure — KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists lifesaving equipment as a trial checkpoint; omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sea trials** = final on-water validation of a boat before delivery. Six phases: Static → In-Shelter → Measured-Mile → Manoeuvring → Stability → Endurance.
- **Speed unit** = KNOTS. Conversion: $1\,\text{kn} = 0.5144\,\text{m/s} = 1.852\,\text{km/h}$.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ — weight of displaced water = weight of boat.
- **Metacentric height:** $GM = KB + BM - KG$. Positive $GM$ = stable; negative $GM$ = capsizes.
- **Righting lever:** $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$. For small angles, $\tan\phi \approx \phi$ in radians.
- **Heel from weight shift:** $\tan\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{\Delta \cdot g \cdot GM}$.
- **Turning circle:** Tactical diameter $T_d \approx 2R$. Advance $A_d = R \cdot \sin\psi$.
- **Stopping distance (approx):** $s_{\text{stop}} = V^2 / (2a)$.
- **Block coefficient** $C_B$ for traditional hulls typically lies between **0.55 and 0.70**.
- **Trial Report must include:** speeds, RPMs, fuel/energy consumed, turning-circle diagram, stability $GZ$ curve, heel angles, bilge-seepage rate, and signatures of master shipwright + owner.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** sea-trial compliance with **Inland Waterways & State Maritime safety regulations** — not just academic speed records.
- **Traditional context matters:** always mention the *type of traditional boat* (*kettuvallam*, *vallam*, *uru*, *odam*) in descriptive answers — KTU values craft-specific terminology.
- **Mandatory checks before handover:** life jackets, oars/paddles, bailer, anchor, navigation lamp, registration number — failing these = certificate withheld.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST864 - TRADITIONAL BOAT BUILDING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Modifications* in traditional boat building refer to the deliberate structural, dimensional, material, and design alterations made to indigenous craft (such as *Vallam*, *Dhow*, *Uru*, *Dhoni*, *Kattumaram*, and country crafts) to enhance their hydrodynamic performance, structural integrity, seaworthiness, cargo capacity, and adaptability to modern operational demands — while preserving the essence of vernacular naval architecture.

In simpler terms, **modifications are the "tune-ups" of a traditional boat** — just as a mechanic upgrades an old car engine with a new fuel injector, a traditional boat builder reshapes the hull, swaps the sail for a diesel engine, or replaces coir rope rigging with synthetic fibre to make the vessel faster, safer, and more durable.

### 1.1 Why Are Modifications Necessary?
Traditional boats were designed centuries ago for specific local conditions — calm backwaters, monsoon winds, or riverine trade. With changing needs, these vessels require modification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Drivers of Modification in KTU Context:**
> - **Performance Upgradation:** Better speed, stability, and fuel efficiency.
> - **Material Substitution:** Replacing scarce/expensive traditional materials (e.g., teak, jungle wood) with cost-effective alternatives (plywood, FRP, aluminium).
> - **Regulatory Compliance:** Meeting modern maritime safety codes (load lines, life-saving appliances).
> - **Operational Shift:** Conversion from sailing to motorized propulsion.
> - **Economic Viability:** Reducing construction time and cost.

### 1.2 Categories of Modifications (Overview)
A high-level taxonomy used in KTU valuation:

| S.No | Modification Type | Primary Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hull Form Modification | Shape, curvature, entry angle |
| 2 | Structural Modification | Frame, plank, keel reinforcement |
| 3 | Material Modification | Wood → Plywood → FRP transition |
| 4 | Propulsion Modification | Sail → Outboard → Inboard engine |
| 5 | Outfit Modification | Deck, cabin, fittings, rigging |
| 6 | Functional Modification | Passenger, fishing, cargo conversion |

**Standard Metric Reference:** Displacement-to-Length Ratio (DLR) for traditional crafts typically ranges between **150 to 350**, with modifications aimed at lowering this for planing-type hulls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Boat Modifications

Modification decisions in traditional boat building follow a structured engineering logic. Each modification can be mapped to one of the following physical principles:

- **Archimedes' Principle** — governs buoyancy changes when hull form is altered.
- **Bernoulli's Equation** — explains water-flow behaviour over modified hull lines.
- **Newton's Second Law** — applied to propulsion modification (engine thrust vs. resistance).
- **Hooke's Law** — relevant for structural frame modifications under load.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Hull Form Modification

1. **Identify the Operational Defect** — e.g., excessive rolling, low speed, bow hammering.
2. **Diagnose the Hydrodynamic Cause** — e.g., too-flat buttocks, insufficient deadrise.
3. **Select Modification Vector** — e.g., increase rocker, add chine, flare the bow.
4. **Quantify the Change** — using offsets, station lines, and design ratios.
5. **Implement and Test** — model testing or full-scale trials.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Oriented)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of the `|` symbol in exam-style tables to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| S.No | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Displacement ($\Delta$) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$ | Total weight of water displaced | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | Block Coefficient ($C_B$) | $C_B = \dfrac{V}{\nabla \cdot L_{WL}}$ | Hull fullness | dimensionless |
| 3 | Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$) | $C_P = \dfrac{V}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$ | Longitudinal distribution of volume | dimensionless |
| 4 | Length-Displacement Ratio (LDR) | $LDR = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{\sqrt[3]{\Delta}}$ | Speed potential indicator | $\vert ft \cdot t^{-1/3}\vert$ |
| 5 | Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$) | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{B$ | Stability vs. speed trade-off | dimensionless |
| 6 | Slenderness Ratio (Beam) | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{WL}}{B}$ | Stability vs. speed trade-off | dimensionless |
| 7 | Speed-Length Ratio (SLR) | $SLR = \dfrac{V_{kn}}{\sqrt{L_{WL}}}$ | Hull speed classification | dimensionless |
| 8 | Righting Moment (RM) | $RM = W \cdot GZ$ | Restoring moment at heel | kN·m |
| 9 | Hull Speed ($V_h$) | $V_h = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Maximum displacement speed | knots |
| 10 | Resistance Increase Factor | $R_{new} = R_{old} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{B,new}}{C_{B,old}}\right)^{2/3}$ | Empirical modification impact | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Question papers frequently ask for *the relationship between LDR, C_B, and SLR* during modification analysis. Memorize the inverse relationship between C_B and speed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Kerala Vallam Modification:** Beach-launched *Vallams* are modified by adding a **chine strake** for better tracking in surf.
- **Uru Conversion:** Traditional Malabar *Urus* are modified by adding a **wheelhouse** and **auxiliary diesel** to comply with fishing licensing norms.
- **Kattumaram Modernization:** The single-log catamaran is modified to twin-outrigger FRP *vallams* for higher catch capacity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Hull Length Modification on Hull Speed

Let a traditional boat of waterline length $L_{WL}$ be extended by a modification factor $k$. The new length is $L_{new} = L_{WL} \cdot (1 + k)$, where $k$ is the fractional length increase (e.g., $k = 0.10$ for 10% extension).

**Step 1: Express the original hull speed.**

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}
$$

**Step 2: Express the modified hull speed.**

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL} \cdot (1 + k)}
$$

**Step 3: Factor out the original length term.**

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}} \cdot \sqrt{(1 + k)}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 1 into Step 3.**

$$
V_{h,new} = V_{h,old} \cdot \sqrt{(1 + k)}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the percentage increase in hull speed.**

$$
\% \Delta V_h = \left(\sqrt{(1 + k)} - 1\right) \cdot 100
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
A traditional *Vallam* of $L_{WL} = 8 \, m$ is extended by $k = 0.21$ (21%). Find the new hull speed.

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 1.34 \cdot 2.828 = 3.79 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
V_{h,new} = 3.79 \cdot \sqrt{1.21} = 3.79 \cdot 1.100 = 4.17 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
\% \Delta V_h = (1.100 - 1) \cdot 100 = 10\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 21% increase in length yields only a 10% increase in hull speed — this is a **non-linear (square-root) relationship** commonly tested in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Derivation: Beam Modification Effect on Stability

When a boat's beam $B$ is increased by modification factor $m$, the transverse metacentric height $GM$ approximately scales with the square of the beam for small-angle stability:

$$
GM \approx \dfrac{I_T}{V} - KB
$$

where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of waterplane area.

For a rectangular approximation:

$$
I_T \approx \dfrac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3}{12}
$$

After beam modification $B_{new} = B \cdot (1 + m)$:

$$
I_{T,new} \approx \dfrac{L_{WL} \cdot B^3 \cdot (1 + m)^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
GM_{new} \approx GM_{old} \cdot (1 + m)^3
$$

This **cubic relationship** is why traditional boat builders carefully avoid excessive beam increase — a small beam change produces a disproportionately large stability gain (or loss, if reduced).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Modification Impact

| Modification | Parameter Affected | Direction of Change | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length Extension | $L_{WL}$ ↑ | $V_h$ ↑ (square root) | Higher hull speed |
| Beam Increase | $B$ ↑ | $GM$ ↑ (cubic) | Greater initial stability |
| Deadrise Increase | Hull V-angle ↑ | Slamming ↓ | Smoother ride in waves |
| Engine Addition | Mass ↑ | Draft ↑ | Reduced freeboard risk |
| FRP Sheathing | Skin mass ↑ | Strength ↑ | Longer hull life |
| Chine Addition | $C_B$ ↑ | Stability ↑ | Planing tendency |
| Rocker Reduction | Curvature ↓ | Tracking ↑ | Less maneuverable |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Modification Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block represents the **decision and execution flow** of a traditional boat modification project — from initial defect identification to final sea trial.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Traditional Boat Baseline] --> B[Operational Need Identified]
    B --> C{Need Category}
    C -->|Performance| D[Hull Form Modification]
    C -->|Strength| E[Structural Modification]
    C -->|Material| F[Material Substitution]
    C -->|Propulsion| G[Powerplant Conversion]
    C -->|Function| H[Outfit and Layout Change]
    D --> D1[Resize LWL, B, T]
    D --> D2[Adjust Cb, Cp, LDR]
    D --> D3[Add chine, flare, sheer]
    E --> E1[Frame reinforcement]
    E --> E2[Keel strengthening]
    E --> E3[Plank thickness change]
    F --> F1[Wood to Plywood]
    F --> F2[Wood to FRP Sheathing]
    F --> F3[Coir to Synthetic Rope]
    G --> G1[Sail to Outboard]
    G --> G2[Sail to Inboard Diesel]
    G --> G3[Hybrid Sail-Engine]
    H --> H1[Open to Cabin]
    H --> H2[Passenger conversion]
    H --> H3[Fishing gear addition]
    D1 --> I[Design Re-Calculation]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Stability and Resistance Check]
    J --> K{Pass Criteria}
    K -->|Yes| L[Construction Modification]
    K -->|No| C
    L --> M[Sea Trial and Validation]
    M --> N[Modified Traditional Boat]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Explanation

- **Node A → B:** The original traditional craft enters the modification pipeline.
- **Node C:** A *decision diamond* classifies the modification need.
- **Sub-nodes (D1–H3):** Represent specific modification actions.
- **Node I:** Central design verification hub — all changes converge here.
- **Node J → K:** Stability and resistance validation; failed designs loop back to C.
- **Node L → M → N:** Final construction, sea trial, and delivery of the **Modified Traditional Boat**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, students are advised to draw such a **flowchart** when asked *"Explain the modification process"* — it fetches full marks under the *diagrammatic representation* component.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Modification" in the context of traditional boat building. List any four types of modifications commonly carried out on indigenous craft.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Modification refers to the deliberate structural, dimensional, or material alterations made to a traditional boat to improve its performance, safety, or operational utility without completely redesigning it from scratch.
**Four types:**
1. Hull form modification (shape, entry angle)
2. Material modification (wood to FRP/plywood)
3. Propulsion modification (sail to engine)
4. Outfit modification (open deck to cabin)

*(Distribution: 1 mark – definition, 2 marks – four types)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is a "chine" in a boat hull? How does the addition of a chine act as a modification to a traditional round-bottom hull?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **chine** is the longitudinal line formed where the side of the hull meets the bottom panels at a distinct angle, replacing the smooth curve of a round-bottom hull.
When added to a traditional round-bottom craft (like a *Vallam*), a chine provides:
- Greater lateral resistance (better tracking)
- Increased transverse stability
- A flatter planing surface at higher speeds

*(Distribution: 1 mark – definition, 2 marks – modification effect)*

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark per missing unit**.
> - In derivation questions, skipping the substitution step leads to **loss of 2–3 marks**.
> - For modification classification questions, students write *one-word answers* (e.g., "Hull form") without **justification** — always explain *what* is modified and *why*.
> - Do not confuse **modification** with **modernization** — modification = alteration of existing craft; modernization = redesign.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hull Form & Structural Modifications

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **hull form modifications** carried out on traditional Kerala boats. Discuss how each modification affects the hydrodynamic performance using the relevant dimensionless coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional fishing *Vallam* of waterline length $L_{WL} = 7 \, m$ and block coefficient $C_B = 0.55$ is modified by increasing its length to $9 \, m$ while keeping the volume approximately the same.
(i) Calculate the new block coefficient.
(ii) Comment on the effect of this modification on the **Speed-Length Ratio** and **hull speed**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hull Form Modifications (7 Marks):**
1. **Length Extension (1 Mark):** Increases $L_{WL}$ → reduces $C_B$ for the same volume → improves SLR and hull speed.
2. **Beam Increase (1 Mark):** Increases $B$ → increases $C_B$ → increases $GM$ cubically → better initial stability.
3. **Deadrise Modification (1 Mark):** Increasing V-angle reduces slamming and improves seakeeping in choppy seas.
4. **Rocker Adjustment (1 Mark):** Reducing rocker (flatter keel line) improves directional tracking.
5. **Sheer and Flare Modification (1 Mark):** Increases freeboard at bow, reducing wet deck in head seas.
6. **Chine and Spray Rail Addition (1 Mark):** Provides lateral resistance and deflects spray.
7. **Transom Modification (1 Mark):** Converting double-ender to transom stern allows inboard engine installation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Use the block coefficient relation: $C_B = \dfrac{V}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$

Since volume $V$ is constant, and assuming $B$ and $T$ remain unchanged:

$$
C_{B,new} = C_{B,old} \cdot \dfrac{L_{WL,old}}{L_{WL,new}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
C_{B,new} = 0.55 \cdot \dfrac{7}{9}
$$

$$
C_{B,new} = 0.55 \cdot 0.7778 = 0.428
$$

**Step 3 (i):** Therefore, the **new block coefficient is $C_B \approx 0.43$** (a slimmer, faster hull form). **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 (ii):** Compute old and new hull speeds:

$$
V_{h,old} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{7} = 1.34 \cdot 2.646 = 3.55 \; \text{knots}
$$

$$
V_{h,new} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{9} = 1.34 \cdot 3.000 = 4.02 \; \text{knots}
$$

**Step 5:** Percentage increase in hull speed:

$$
\% \Delta V_h = \dfrac{4.02 - 3.55}{3.55} \cdot 100 = 13.24\%
$$

**Step 6:** New Speed-Length Ratio:

$$
SLR_{old} = \dfrac{3.55}{\sqrt{7}} = 1.342
$$

$$
SLR_{new} = \dfrac{4.02}{\sqrt{9}} = 1.340
$$

*(Note: SLR is mathematically fixed at 1.34 for displacement hulls at hull speed — the modification now allows the boat to *reach* this SLR more efficiently.)*

**Conclusion (2 Marks):** The modification produces a **13% gain in hull speed** with a slimmer hull form (lower $C_B$), confirming that length extension is one of the most effective hull form modifications for speed enhancement in traditional craft.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Material & Propulsion Modifications *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** Discuss the **material modifications** traditionally adopted in boat building — from solid wood to plywood to FRP. Compare their advantages and limitations in the context of Kerala's traditional crafts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A traditional sailing *Dhow* of displacement $\Delta = 8$ tonnes is converted to a motorized vessel by installing a 25 HP outboard engine. The engine weighs **95 kg** and the auxiliary fuel tank adds **40 kg**. The original crew + cargo load was 600 kg. Determine the **new displacement** and the **percentage increase in draft**, assuming the waterplane area remains constant at $A_W = 12 \, m^2$ and the seawater density is $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Material Modifications (7 Marks):**
1. **Solid Wood → Marine Plywood (2 Marks):**
   - *Advantages:* Reduced cost, uniform strength, less warping, easy availability.
   - *Limitations:* Susceptible to delamination, requires epoxy sealing.
2. **Plywood → FRP (Fibreglass) (2 Marks):**
   - *Advantages:* Waterproof, low maintenance, high strength-to-weight ratio, easy moulding.
   - *Limitations:* High initial mould cost, poor reparability, environmental concerns.
3. **Coir Rope → Synthetic Fibre (1 Mark):**
   - Higher tensile strength, rot resistance, longer life.
4. **Iron Fastenings → Stainless Steel / Copper (1 Mark):**
   - Corrosion resistance, reduced staining of timber.
5. **Lead Ballast → Concrete or Iron Pig (1 Mark):**
   - Cost reduction, local availability.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert engine and fuel tank mass to tonnes:

$$
m_{engine} = 95 \; kg = 0.095 \; t
$$

$$
m_{fuel} = 40 \; kg = 0.040 \; t
$$

**Step 2:** Compute new displacement:

$$
\Delta_{new} = \Delta_{old} + m_{engine} + m_{fuel} + m_{crew+cargo}
$$

$$
\Delta_{new} = 8 + 0.095 + 0.040 + 0.6
$$

$$
\Delta_{new} = 8.735 \; t
$$

**Step 3:** Convert displacement to newtons ($1 \, t \approx 9.81 \, kN$):

$$
W_{new} = 8.735 \cdot 9.81 = 85.69 \; kN
$$

**Step 4:** Compute original draft (for reference, original $W_{old}$ in kN):

$$
W_{old} = (8 + 0.6) \cdot 9.81 = 84.366 \; kN
$$

**Step 5:** Use $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_W \cdot T$ to find drafts:

$$
T_{old} = \dfrac{W_{old}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_W} = \dfrac{84366}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 12}
$$

$$
T_{old} = \dfrac{84366}{120651} = 0.699 \; m
$$

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{W_{new}}{1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 12} = \dfrac{85690}{120651} = 0.710 \; m
$$

**Step 6:** Percentage increase in draft:

$$
\% \Delta T = \dfrac{0.710 - 0.699}{0.699} \cdot 100 = 1.57\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The propulsion modification increases displacement by **9.2%** but draft increases by only **1.57%** due to the large waterplane area, confirming the boat's suitability for engine retrofitting without major redesign.

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Modifications" (Module 4)**
>
> - **Definition:** Alterations to traditional craft for performance/safety/economic gain.
> - **6 Major Types:** Hull form, Structural, Material, Propulsion, Outfit, Functional.
> - **Key Coefficients to Memorize:** $C_B$, $C_P$, LDR, SLR, $\lambda$ (Slenderness).
> - **Hull Speed Formula:** $V_h = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ in knots.
> - **Beam–Stability Relation:** $GM \propto B^3$ (cubic, very sensitive).
> - **Length–Speed Relation:** $V_h \propto \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ (square-root, less sensitive).
> - **Material Evolution Path:** Solid wood → Plywood → FRP composite.
> - **Propulsion Evolution Path:** Paddle → Sail → Outboard → Inboard → Hybrid.
> - **Kerala Specials:** Vallam chine addition, Uru wheelhouse retrofit, Kattumaram to twin-outrigger FRP.
> - **Always cite units in numerical answers** — major KTU mark-deduction point.
> - **Modification ≠ Modernization** — modification alters existing craft; modernization is a full redesign.
